MINOR PROPHETS

VOL. II

Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai,


PREFACE

This is our second volume on the Minor Prophets in the Bible Believer's Commentary Series. Volume one covered the books of Hosea--Nahum; this volume will cover the books of Habakkuk--Malachi.

[Volume one was first published in 1978. Volume two was put off for many years for reasons expressed in my prefatory remarks on pages v and vi of volume one. There are certain passages, especially in Zechariah, where we encounter an undue amount of trouble in exposition. My people here at the Bible Baptist Church in Pensacola, Florida, finally persuaded me to present what I do know about the five books, even though there are several places where the only comment I will be able to make is ]{.class-0}[Ich weiss nicht]{.class-1}[ (German for "I don't know"; see the ]{.class-0}[Bible Believer's Commentary on the Song of Solomon]{.class-1}[).]{.class-0}

[I have never professed to understand all of the Bible, nor have I ever claimed to be a Bible ]{.class-0}[scholar.]{.class-1}[ I am a ]{.class-0}[student]{.class-1}[ of the Bible, and as a student, I am still learning many things.]{.class-0}

[When we come to those passages I do not completely understand, I will tell you so. I will give you what I can on the passage, but I will not "bluff my way through" as the other commentators do. I will not run to the "original Hebrew" or the ]{.class-0}[Septuagint]{.class-1}[ to make you think I know about what I'm talking when I don't.]{.class-0}

[As I stated in the Preface to volume one, where we have trouble interpreting a verse or passage, we will ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ do the following things:]{.class-0}

[1. We will not ]{.class-0}[add words]{.class-1}[ to the text so it will make sense.]{.class-0}

[2. We will not ]{.class-0}[subtract words]{.class-1}[ from the text so it will make sense.]{.class-0}

[3. We will not ]{.class-0}[correct the English]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}[text]{.class-1}[ with ]{.class-0}[any]{.class-1}[ Greek or Hebrew because we are too stupid to understand what God said in the ]{.class-0}[AV.]{.class-1}

[4. We will not ]{.class-0}[complain]{.class-1}[ about the ]{.class-0}[Authorized]{.class-1}[ text being ]{.class-0}[corrupt]{.class-1}[ to cover up our own stupidity.]{.class-0}

[5. We are not going to ]{.class-0}[accept any man's word]{.class-1}[ on the meaning of ]{.class-0}[any]{.class-1}[ verse or passage when that man ]{.class-0}[casts doubt on]{.class-1}[ or ]{.class-0}[doesn't believe]{.class-1}[ the ]{.class-0}[King James]{.class-1}[ text.]{.class-0}

[We will take the stance we have taken in all our works in the Bible Believer's Commentary Series: "Where the Bible says one thing and scholarship says another, ]{.class-0}[scholarship]{.class-1}[ can go plumb to the Devil" (Billy Sunday, 1936). We will not change what God said in the ]{.class-0}[AV]{.class-1}[ for ]{.class-0}[any]{.class-1}[ scholar---Liberal or Conservative. Our position is where the Bible (]{.class-0}[AV]{.class-1}[, 1611) speaks, "Christian" scholarship can shut its mouth and learn. In this series of commentaries, it is the Conservative and Liberal scholars who are on trial, not ]{.class-0}["the holy scriptures"]{.class-2}[ (Rom. 1:2).]{.class-0}

[Now, when it comes to an introduction to the Minor Prophets, we refer you to our remarks in volume one for a detailed study. Here we will just remind you that the Minor Prophets are ]{.class-0}[true prophets;]{.class-1}[ i.e., they give future predictions of what will happen ]{.class-0}[in history.]{.class-1}

[Any one of the Minor Prophets will give you more actual ]{.class-0}[prophecy]{.class-1}[ than you will find in all 114 "Suras" of Mohammed's ]{.class-0}[Koran.]{.class-1}[ Outside of a couple of lucky guesses on the outcome of a battle (a 50% chance of being right: you will either win or you will lose), Mohammed couldn't give you ]{.class-0}[one]{.class-1}[ prophecy of ]{.class-0}[anything]{.class-1}[ that took place in ]{.class-0}[time]{.class-1}[ in ]{.class-0}[history]{.class-1}[ (not at the "Last Judgment" when "time is no more," Rev. 10:6). That is Allah's "prophet"; you could get more "prophecy" out of a fortune cookie.]{.class-0}

[The main theme of the Minor Prophets is the main theme of the Old Testament and, for that matter,]{.class-0}[ the entire Bible:]{.class-1}[ it is the Second Advent of the Lord Jesus Christ. In this volume, we will see the ]{.class-0}[route]{.class-1}[ of the Second Advent in Habakkuk, the ]{.class-0}[negative nature]{.class-1}[ of the Second Advent in Zephaniah, the ]{.class-0}[rebuilding of the Temple]{.class-1}[ connected with the Second Advent in Haggai, the ]{.class-0}[final battle]{.class-1}[ of the Second Advent in Zechariah, and ]{.class-0}[warnings]{.class-1}[ preceding the Second Advent in Malachi.]{.class-0}

[The Minor Prophets are distinguished from the Major Prophets (Isaiah--Daniel) because of their ]{.class-0}[size,]{.class-1}[ not the contents of their message. Because of this, in the Hebrew Bible, all twelve books are grouped together as one. But they are in no way inferior to anything written by Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, or Daniel. As part of ]{.class-0}["all scriptures,"]{.class-2}[ they are as ]{.class-0}["profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness"]{.class-2}[ (2 Tim. 3:16) as any other part of the word of God.]{.class-0}

[So as we take up the exposition of the last five books of the Old Testament, we will not treat them lightly. We will let the Author of the Book (2 Pet. 1:21) lead and guide us ]{.class-0}["into all truth"]{.class-2}[ (John 16:13), always being careful to ]{.class-0}["let God be true, but every man a liar"]{.class-2}[ (Rom. 3:4).]{.class-0}

[]{#section-0_1_16643674dab7db5b_b681.xhtml}

INTRODUCTION TO

HABAKKUK

[Habakkuk is one of six undated books in the Minor Prophets (the others being Joel, Obadiah, Jonah, Nahum, and Malachi). By "undated" we mean that there is no specific King or event mentioned in the book to indicate when the author wrote. In fact, when it comes to any personal information about him, Habakkuk is a "dark horse." There is nothing in the entire Bible that tells you who he was, from where he was, or what his family or tribe was (although some commentators have ]{.class-0}[speculated]{.class-1}[ that he was part of the "Levitical choir" based on the "psalm" of chapter 3).]{.class-0}

Most expositors (including Scofield) place him at the latter part of Josiah's reign. If that is so, then he was contemporary with Jeremiah (Jer. 1:2) and Zephaniah (Zeph. 1:1). Archbishop Ussher gives a date of 626 B.C. for the book.

[Habakkuk has 3 chapters, 56 verses, and 1,475 words. His name is a reference to a fragrant herb and can roughly be translated as "love's embrace" or "ardently embraced." The picture is somebody giving the one they love a great big hug. If you can find ]{.class-0}[that]{.class-1}[ in the book of Habakkuk, then I am a monkey's uncle. Like most of the Minor Prophets, Habakkuk is 90% ]{.class-0}[negative.]{.class-1}

[By modern, critical scholastic standards, the book is considered "non-productive." The apostate Jews of ]{.class-0}[The Jewish Encyclopedia]{.class-1}[ have the theme of Habakkuk being "the return of God's compassion." Unfortunately for them, the book contains a complete description of the Second Advent of Christ, including a repeat of what took place in Joshua 10:12 (cf. Hab. 3:11). In Habakkuk 3, you find an account of the entire Jewish nation being saved by their Messiah, with Genesis 3:15 being fulfilled while He's doing it (cf. Hab. 3:13). Yet, no Rabbi in the entire 14,000 pages of ]{.class-0}[The Jewish Encyclopedia]{.class-1}[ could find one reference in Habakkuk's prophecy to either the First or Second Advents of his Messiah (see the comments on Hab. 1:5). They place chapter 3 back in the past at the giving of the Law (Exod. 20), and they get rid of the future fulfillment of Genesis 3:15 (see comments on Rom. 16:20 in that Commentary) by retranslating ]{.class-0}["thou woundest the head"]{.class-2}[ (Hab. 3:13) as "laying him bare from thigh to neck."]{.class-0}

[Nor do the Gentile "Christians" fare any better. Habakkuk 3 is sung as a liturgical psalm without any literal ]{.class-0}[belief]{.class-1}[ in its words. If the scholars don't treat it as past history, then they allegorize it and spiritualize it to get rid of its prophetic meaning.]{.class-0}

[Our approach to Habakkuk will be the same as it has been in every book in the Bible Believer's Commentary Series: we will accept the ]{.class-0}[King James]{.class-1}[ text as it says it, where it says it, and we will not change one word in order to "curry favor" with anyone in the Scholars' Union. We will accept Habakkuk's prophecy as literally true without spiritualizing anything away to force it to match something in the New Testament we know to be so. We will let Scripture interpret Scripture and dump the scholars whenever they dump the Holy Bible (]{.class-0}[AV]{.class-1}[ 1611) for the "originals" or "better translations."]{.class-0}

[]{#section-0_1_531795237f8f40f7_b682.xhtml}

CHAPTER ONE

1:1 The burden which Habakkuk the prophet did see.

2 O LORD, how long shall I cry, and thou wilt not hear! even cry out unto thee of violence, and thou wilt not save!

3 Why dost thou shew me iniquity, and cause me to behold grievance? for spoiling and violence are before me: and there are that raise up strife and contention.

4 Therefore the law is slacked, and judgment doth never go forth: for the wicked doth compass about the righteous; therefore wrong judgment proceedeth.

5 Behold ye among the heathen, and regard, and wonder marvellously: for I will work a work in your days, which ye will not believe, though it be told you.

"[The burden which Habakkuk the prophet did see"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 1). Habakkuk starts his prophecy as Nahum starts his: with a ]{.class-0}["burden."]{.class-2}[ In the context, ]{.class-0}["the burden"]{.class-2}[ is "a prophecy of calamitous or disastrous nature" (]{.class-0}[Easton's Bible Dictionary]{.class-1}[), but it can also be defined as "a load of any kind," "a severe task," or "a difficult duty requiring effort." Any minister involved in sound Scriptural preaching knows the truth of those last three statements. If a preacher is doing what he is supposed to be doing behind the pulpit (i.e., exposing sin and calling sinners to repentance), it is not an easy job.]{.class-0}

[I used to have a fishing buddy who was a paint contractor by profession. He used to tease me about preachers being in the ministry because they didn't want to work. I told him he couldn't handle the job I do, and he couldn't. Instead of going into the ministry and actually pastoring a church, he started a little Hyperdispensationalist "Bible study" with his family and any students from ]{.class-0}[our school ]{.class-1}[he could get to listen to him. If you think it is easy getting up and telling people what they are doing wrong week after week, then try it for a while and see how you like it.]{.class-0}

[A real Bible message (not the modern "love," "coping," and "sharing and caring" garbage) is a "burden," brother. Jeremiah got tired of preaching death and destruction all the time with nobody listening to him, and he tried quitting the ministry; he found out that ]{.class-0}["his word was in mine heart as a burning fire shut up in my bones, and I was weary with forbearing, and I could not stay"]{.class-2}[ (Jer. 20:9). That is the idea behind what Habakkuk is saying here.]{.class-0}

"[O LORD, how long shall I cry, and thou wilt not hear! even cry out unto thee of violence, and thou wilt not save"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 2). Habakkuk is in the same boat as Jeremiah (Lam. 3:8) and Job (Job 30:20). This sets the verse ]{.class-0}[doctrinally]{.class-1}[ in the Tribulation (see comments on Job 30:16--24 in that Commentary). In verses 3--4 here in Habakkuk 1, Habakkuk, as Jeremiah and Job, is a picture of the Jew in the Tribulation. The martyrs in the Tribulation pray, ]{.class-0}["How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth?"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 6:10).]{.class-0}

"[Why dost thou shew me iniquity, and cause me to behold grievance?"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 3). In other words, why is the Lord showing no compassion to that Jew, and the answer is that that is what they said they wanted. When Jesus was on trial before Pontius Pilate, the leaders of the people rejected their Messiah as King and ]{.class-0}[wanted]{.class-1}[ a Roman dictator to rule over them (John 19:15), and they ]{.class-0}[accepted]{.class-1}[ the blood guilt for murdering their Messiah:]{.class-0}[ "His blood be on us, AND ON OUR CHILDREN"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 27:25). So for the next 2,000 years that Jew has been run by Roman Catholic Popes, Kings, and dictators. He has had his property confiscated, he has been forced into ghettos, he has been forced to wear a "yellow star" (as far back as the Fourth Lateran Council in 1215; Hitler was just being a good Roman Catholic following the official dictates of his church), he has been exiled (e.g., Spain in 1492), he has been persecuted (Russian Orthodox pogroms), he has been tortured (Spanish Inquisition), and he has been exterminated (Nazi Germany). Why? ]{.class-0}[That's what they said they wanted!]{.class-1}

[Verse 3: ]{.class-0}["for spoiling and violence are before me: and there are that raise up strife and contention."]{.class-2}[ That's what that Jew has been going through for 2,000 years, and the Lord's not through with them yet. Paul said ]{.class-0}["wrath is come upon them to the uttermost"]{.class-2}[ (1 Thess. 2:16). Now that's not ]{.class-0}["completely and forever"]{.class-1}[ as the perverse ]{.class-0}[Amplified Version]{.class-1}[ put out by the Lockman Foundation (the publishers of "Satan's masterpiece," the ]{.class-0}[NASV]{.class-1}[) reads. But you have to admit that 2,000 years is a mighty long time for ]{.class-0}["iniquity," "grievance," "spoiling," "violence," "strife,"]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["contention"]{.class-2}[ against ]{.class-0}[one race]{.class-1}[ of people. And it's not through yet: the Lord is going to allow that nation to be cut down to a population of less than 200,000 before He does anything about it.]{.class-0}

[Now maybe that kind of stuff doesn't bother you, but it bothers me all the time. You see, I ]{.class-0}[read,]{.class-1}[ and I read ]{.class-0}[history]{.class-1}[ and know how those people have been treated for twenty centuries. If I could just quit ]{.class-0}[reading,]{.class-1}[ I would be a normal person. But the more I read about that nation, and the more I study history, the more I get to wondering: "God, why don't you do something? Give them a break; for God's sake, ]{.class-0}[give them a break!"]{.class-1}

I mean, it's like suffering Lou Gehrig's Disease every single day, 24 hours a day, for 2,000 years. That's the condition the nation is in. You would think God and the Devil would both be content with everything they've gone through and say "That's enough," but they don't.

"[Therefore the law is slacked, and judgment doth never go forth"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 4). David said, ]{.class-0}["It is time for thee, LORD, to work: for they have made void thy law"]{.class-2}[ (Psa. 119:126); the idea being that since the heathen are violating what God has said about that nation being His chosen people, it is time for the Lord to ]{.class-0}[do something]{.class-1}[ about it. But that is not what is happening here. The Law is being ]{.class-0}["made void,"]{.class-2}[ and the Lord is letting the wicked get away with it.]{.class-0}

"[For the wicked doth compass about the righteous."]{.class-2}[ That's what happens to David in Psalm 17:9, to Christ at His trial and crucifixion (Psa. 109:3, 22:12, 16), to Israel at the end of the Tribulation (Psa. 17:11, 118:10--12), and to the saints in Jerusalem at the end of the Millennium (Rev. 20:9). As we shall see from verses 5--6, the historical context here is Judah just before the invasion of Nebuchadnezzar, so what you have here is the situation found in Jeremiah 12:6, 26:8, 20--23. There is only a remnant left in Jerusalem and Judah ]{.class-0}["that sigh and that cry for all the abominations that be done in the midst thereof"]{.class-2}[ (see Ezek. 9:4--9).]{.class-0}

"[Therefore wrong judgment proceedeth."]{.class-2}[ The reason ]{.class-0}["the law is slacked"]{.class-2}[ and correct judgments aren't made according to it (cf. Jer. 5:26--28) is because ]{.class-0}["the wicked"]{.class-2}[ are first the ]{.class-0}[religious]{.class-1}[ leaders and ]{.class-0}[then]{.class-1}[ the politicians (see Ezek. 22:26--29). The blame for the condition of America can be laid squarely at the feet of the nation's ]{.class-0}[preachers, pastors, evangelists,]{.class-1}[ and the faculties and staffs of ]{.class-0}[Christian Colleges, Universities, and Seminaries. ]{.class-1}[They were the ones who talked the American populace out of their belief in the Scriptures God gave them (the ]{.class-0}[AV]{.class-1}[ 1611); the politicians simply took advantage of this apostasy and "followed through" on the infidelity.]{.class-0}

[Verse 5: ]{.class-0}["Behold ye among the heathen, and regard, and wonder marvellously: for I will work a work in your days, which ye will not believe, though it be told you."]{.class-2}[ Since this verse is quoted by the Apostle Paul in Acts 13:41, all the Scholars' Union goes to pieces here.]{.class-0}

[The first thing you are told about the passage is that Paul was quoting some mythological B.C. ]{.class-0}[Septuagint.]{.class-1}[ Look at the verse.]{.class-0}

"[Behold, ye despisers, and wonder, and perish: for I work a work in your days, a work which ye shall in no wise believe, though a man declare it unto you"]{.class-2}[ (Acts 13:41).]{.class-0}

[The two passages obviously don't match, so to prevent Paul from "misquoting" Scripture, the Scholars' Union (Knowling, Ladd, Robertson, Clarke, Dummelow, Lange, Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown, et al.) insist Paul quoted an uncial manuscript written in A.D. 440 (]{.class-0}[Alexandrinus]{.class-1}[), which is backed up by two other uncial manuscripts (]{.class-0}[Sinaiticus]{.class-1}[ and]{.class-0}[ Vaticanus]{.class-1}[) written more than 200 years after the completion of the New Testament.]{.class-0}

[What has happened in Acts 13:41 is that Paul has made a ]{.class-0}[free quotation]{.class-1}[ of Habakkuk 1:5 in order to apply it to his audience in Antioch of Pisidia. This was done under the leadership of the ]{.class-0}[Holy Spirit,]{.class-1}[ the real Author of Habakkuk 1:5 (cf. 2 Pet. 1:21), who is allowed to quote and apply His own work as He sees fit (a right afforded to the author of any work). The compilers of the ]{.class-0}[Septuagint]{.class-1}[ (Origen, Symmachus, Theodotian, Aquila, et al.)---none of whom lived before the birth of Christ---simply took what Luke wrote and ]{.class-0}[stuck it back]{.class-1}[ into Habakkuk 1:5 (for a more complete discussion of this, see the comments on Acts 13:41 in that Commentary).]{.class-0}

The second thing into which you run with this verse is the attempt to force it into a New Testament doctrinal setting involving justification by grace through faith without the works of the Law. Scofield says Acts 13:41 interprets Habakkuk 1:5 as a "prediction of the redemptive work of Christ," specifically to the Jews of the Dispersion (see his footnote on Hab. 1:5).

[It most certainly is NOT]{.class-1}[. The Pauline ]{.class-0}["gospel of the grace of God"]{.class-2}[ (Acts 20:24) isn't anywhere in Habakkuk 1. All Paul has done in Acts 13:41 is to reach back into the Old Testament and pull out Habakkuk 1:5 as a prooftext for the point he is making on New Testament salvation by grace. You will see him do this continually in his epistles (see the comments on Rom. 10:15 in that Commentary).]{.class-0}

[Paul makes what is known as a ]{.class-0}[practical]{.class-1}[ application; it has nothing to do with the ]{.class-0}[doctrinal]{.class-1}[ or ]{.class-0}[historical]{.class-1}[ context of Habakkuk 1. I have often said that, sometimes, good doctrine does not make good preaching and that good preaching does not always make good doctrine. Now what do I mean by that?]{.class-0}

[Well, the Scripture is not only ]{.class-0}["profitable for doctrine,"]{.class-2}[ but also ]{.class-0}["for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness"]{.class-2}[ (2 Tim. 3:16). Every verse in the Bible has ]{.class-0}[three]{.class-1}[ applications. The first is a ]{.class-0}[historical]{.class-1}[ application; i.e., the verse was written to a specific person or people at a specific time in history and is ]{.class-0}[true historically.]{.class-1}[ It is not a "legend" or a "myth" or a "fable" made up to "teach some spiritual truth." The verse is telling the truth about something that happened the way the verse said it happened. It's not a question of "faith" or "belief"; it ]{.class-0}[took place.]{.class-1}[ The Bible is first and foremost a ]{.class-0}[history]{.class-1}[ book, the only ]{.class-0}[completely accurate]{.class-1}[ history book on which you will ever get your hands.]{.class-0}

[Next, a verse has a ]{.class-0}[doctrinal]{.class-1}[ application. It teaches you what is ]{.class-0}[so]{.class-1}[ and what is ]{.class-0}[not so ]{.class-1}[for you to believe. Quite often, the doctrinal application deals with ]{.class-0}[prophecy.]{.class-1}

[Finally, all Scripture has a ]{.class-0}[spiritual]{.class-1}[ application, meaning you can apply a verse to you ]{.class-0}[devotionally]{.class-1}[ or ]{.class-0}[practically]{.class-1}[ even though it was not written to you historically or meant for you doctrinally.]{.class-0}

[Now, every verse in the Bible is like that. When Paul quotes Habakkuk 1:5, he uses the verse ]{.class-0}[devotionally.]{.class-1}[ He is saying that just as the people of Judah didn't believe what was given to them in Habakkuk 1, the Jews to whom he was speaking were going to miss salvation by grace through faith in Jesus Christ because they wouldn't believe what Paul was preaching. ]{.class-0}[That is found nowhere in the context of Habakkuk 1]{.class-1}[. Historically, the reference is to the invasion of Nebuchadnezzar (see vs. 6), and since Nebuchadnezzar is a type of the Antichrist (see the comments on vs. 11), some doctrinal application can be made to the events preceding the Second Advent.]{.class-0}

Now, if what Paul did seems strange to you, just remember how many times you (if you are a soulwinner) have used this verse:

"[Behold, I stand at the door, and knock: if any man hear my voice, and open the door, I will come in to him, and will sup with him, and he with me."]{.class-2}

[That's Revelation 3:20. I have used ]{.class-0}[that]{.class-1}[ verse in personal work, and I will continue to use it. But if you look at the context of that verse, it has nothing to do with Jesus Christ coming into anybody's heart or the Holy Spirit taking up residence in anyone's body or God ]{.class-0}[saving]{.class-1}[ anybody. The verse is written to a church, and Christ is ]{.class-0}[outside the church with the door shut to Him.]{.class-1}[ If anybody in the church hears Him knocking at the door asking to come in, he has to go open the door for the Lord because the church ]{.class-0}[doesn't want Him in there.]{.class-1}[ If a guy will do that, Christ will come into that church and have fellowship with ]{.class-0}[that]{.class-1}[ fellow. Historically, that's written to the Laodicean church, a First-Century church in Asia Minor; ]{.class-0}[doctrinally]{.class-1}[ that's written to a bunch of Christians just before the Rapture of the Church. But you can make a spiritual application to a lost man receiving Jesus Christ as his Saviour; it makes good devotional teaching for personal work. That is the kind of thing Paul is doing in Acts 13:41.]{.class-0}

[Finally, note the cross-reference to verse 5 in Isaiah 29:14. The unbelief of Habakkuk 1:5 is caused by the ]{.class-0}[scholars]{.class-1}[ (]{.class-0}["the wisdom of their wise men shall perish, and the understanding of their prudent men shall be hid"]{.class-2}[). The infidelity passed on by these ]{.class-0}["wise"]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["prudent"]{.class-2}[ (see Matt. 11:25; Luke 10:21; 1 Cor. 1:19) is caused by substituting ]{.class-0}[men's traditions]{.class-1}[ for ]{.class-0}[what God said]{.class-1}[ (Isa. 29:13 cf. Mark 7:7--13).]{.class-0}

[It was Charles Haddon Spurgeon who said: "To some, the teaching of Scripture is not of final authority: the fount of inspiration is not within the Book and with the Holy Spirit, but within ]{.class-0}[man's own intelligence.]{.class-1}[ The ]{.class-0}[ministers]{.class-1}[ have preached the people ]{.class-0}[out of their faith in the Scriptures; they]{.class-1}[ taught them to be ]{.class-0}[doubters."]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}

[That is exactly the reason for the state America is in today. ]{.class-0}[The Christian Colleges, Seminaries, and Universities taught their ministerial students to doubt the Book (AV 1611); and when those students became pastors, evangelists, and Bible teachers, they taught their congregations to do the same.]{.class-1}[ As a result, you have churches all across this country packed from the pulpit to the pew with people whose final authority is no higher than their own feelings, opinions, and preferences: they are their own gods. So even though the Lord has clearly stated what will happen to any nation that lives like Americans live (see Psa. 9:17), they don't believe it.]{.class-0}

6 For, lo, I raise up the Chaldeans, that bitter and hasty nation, which shall march through the breadth of the land, to possess the dwelling-places that are not theirs.

7 They are terrible and dreadful: their judgment and their dignity shall proceed of themselves.

8 Their horses also are swifter than the leopards, and are more fierce than the evening wolves: and their horsemen shall spread themselves, and their horsemen shall come from far; they shall fly as the eagle that hasteth to eat.

9 They shall come all for violence: their faces shall sup up as the east wind, and they shall gather the captivity as the sand.

10 And they shall scoff at the kings, and the princes shall be a scorn unto them: they shall deride every strong hold; for they shall heap dust, and take it.

11 Then shall his mind change, and he shall pass over, and offend, imputing this his power unto his god.

"[For, lo, I raise up the Chaldeans"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 6). That's the context of verse 5. There's nothing about Christ dying for anybody; there is not the remotest connection to Acts 13.]{.class-0}

"[The Chaldeans"]{.class-2}[ are the Babylonians under Nebuchadnezzar (Ezra 5:12). The kingdom was founded by Nimrod back in Genesis 10:8--11 (cf. Isa. 23:13). Today it's the country of Iraq, so historically, you are now back at the place where you started.]{.class-0}

"[That bitter and hasty nation, which shall march through the breadth of the land, to possess the dwelling places that are not theirs. They are terrible and dreadful\...Their horses also are swifter than the leopards, and are more fierce than the evening wolves\...their horsemen shall come from far; they shall fly as the eagle that hasteth to eat\....they shall deride every strong hold; for they shall heap dust, and take it"]{.class-2}[ (vss. 6--10).]{.class-0}

This is a fulfillment of what God told the Jews He would do to them if they didn't keep the Law when they dwelt in the Promised Land.

"[The LORD shall bring a nation against thee from far, from the end of the earth, as swift as the eagle flieth\...A nation of fierce countenance\...he shall eat the fruit of thy cattle, and the fruit of thy land, until thou be destroyed\...he shall besiege thee in all thy gates, until thy high and fenced walls come down, wherein thou trustedst, throughout all thy land"]{.class-2}[ (Deut. 28:49--52).]{.class-0}

[He says in Ezekiel 7:24, ]{.class-0}["Wherefore I will bring the WORST of the heathen, and they shall possess their houses: I will also make the pomp of the strong to cease; and their holy places shall be defiled."]{.class-2}

[Now, who are these ]{.class-0}["Chaldeans"]{.class-2}[ today who are a ]{.class-0}["bitter and hasty nation",]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["the worst of the heathen"]{.class-2}[? They're ]{.class-0}[Moslems.]{.class-1}[ Every native in Iraq, as well as Iran, is a ]{.class-0}[Moslem.]{.class-1}

[Now, when you get to verse 8, you run into some ]{.class-0}["horses"]{.class-2}[ that are connected with Satan (]{.class-0}["wolves"]{.class-2}[---see John 10:12; Acts 20:29) and the Antichrist (]{.class-0}["leopards"]{.class-2}[---see Rev. 13:2). "[They shall fly as the eagle that hasteth to eat"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 8). This is a reference to someone flying---]{.class-0}[literally]{.class-1}[---as you find it in Matthew 24:20 and Revelation 12:14 (see comments on those verses in their respective Commentaries). You say, "Then why didn't the Lord say 'they shall fly as the B-17s or the F-15s or the Messerschmitts or the Harriers or the Tomcats'---or something like that?" Because if He had, nobody for the next 2,000 years could have understood about what He was talking. There weren't any planes flying around in 600 B.C. or 500 B.C. or 400 B.C. or A.D. 100, 200, 300, 400, etc. But everyone from 600 B.C. to A.D. 1900 could understand ]{.class-0}["fly as the eagle."]{.class-2}

"[They shall come all for violence"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 9). Deuteronomy 28:50 said the Chaldeans wouldn't ]{.class-0}["regard the person of the old, nor shew favour to the young." ]{.class-2}[Second Chronicles 36:17 says Nebuchadnezzar ]{.class-0}["had no compassion upon young men or maiden, old man, or him that stooped for age."]{.class-2}[ According to Psalm 137:8--9, the Babylonian soldiers took Jewish babies and bashed their brains out on the rocks. Jeremiah says the women were raped and the princes were strung up by their hands (Lam. 5:11--12).]{.class-0}

"[Their faces shall sup up as the east wind."]{.class-2}[ The Hebrew scholars have a hard time with the expression ]{.class-0}["sup up"]{.class-2}[ (see the ]{.class-0}[Theological Word Book of the Old Testament,]{.class-1}[ Vol. 1, pg. 167). Some of the guesses as to its meaning are "to hoard" or "to be in opposition." But all of this etymological speculation is quite unnecessary. The Bible believer doesn't need a knowledge of Hebrew or Greek to understand the Bible; the key to Scriptural interpretation is ]{.class-0}[Scripture with Scripture]{.class-1}[ (see 1 Cor. 2:13).]{.class-0}

[In the Old Testament, Hebrew sentences are so constructed that the same information is repeated in a different way. This ]{.class-0}[double way]{.class-1}[ of saying the same thing (see the principle laid down in Gen. 41:32) is found over and over again in the Old Testament, especially in the "wisdom" books (e.g., Job 3:11--18, 24--26, 4:8--11, 5:2--5, 9:11, 13--14; Prov. 1:13--15, 20--21, 24--26, 30--31). Southerners retained this habit of speech because of their exposure to the Bible: "Boy, it sure is a hot day; yes sir, it sure is a hot one." "I liked to a had a time of it, man; I mean, a time of it." "Yeah, that fish is a beauty; yes sir, he sure is a beaut."]{.class-0}

[The phrase ]{.class-0}["sup up"]{.class-2}[ is defined right in the same verse as ]{.class-0}["gather,"]{.class-2}[ just as ]{.class-0}["scoff"]{.class-2}[ in verse 10 matches ]{.class-0}["deride"]{.class-2}[ in that verse. By comparing Scripture with Scripture, the verse means that Nebuchadnezzar and his men are going to ]{.class-0}[eat up]{.class-1}[ everything the Jews have, just as the Midianites and the Amalekites did in Judges 6:3--5. Again, this is exactly what Moses prophesied 900 years earlier.]{.class-0}

"[And he shall eat the fruit of thy cattle, and the fruit of thy land, until thou be destroyed: which also shall not leave thee neither corn, wine, or oil, or the increase of thy kine, or flocks of thy sheep, until he have destroyed thee"]{.class-2}[ (Deut. 28:51).]{.class-0}

[Back to verse 9: ]{.class-0}["Their faces shall sup up as the east wind." "The east wind"]{.class-2}[ is a bad wind in the Scriptures (see Job 15:2, 27:21, 38:24; Psa. 48:7; Isa. 27:8; Jer. 18:17; Ezek. 17:10, 19:12, 27:26; Hos. 12:1, 13:15). In the Mediterranean area, it is called a "sirocco" or a "Syrian wind." It was ]{.class-0}["the east wind"]{.class-2}[ that blasted the ]{.class-0}["seven thin ears"]{.class-2}[ in Pharaoh's dream which warned of the seven years of famine that were to come (Gen. 41:6, 23, 27). It was ]{.class-0}["an east wind"]{.class-2}[ that brought in the plague of locusts on Egypt (Exod. 10:13). It was ]{.class-0}["a vehement east wind"]{.class-2}[ that caused Jonah to pray for God to kill him (Jon. 4:8). The only place in the Bible where an east wind is good is when the Lord divided the Red Sea ]{.class-0}["by a strong east wind"]{.class-2}[ (Exod. 14:21; Psa. 78:26). When an east wind blows, fishermen don't go out to fish.]{.class-0}

"[They shall gather the captivity as the sands"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 9). You will remember that God promised to multiply Abraham's seed ]{.class-0}["as the sand which is upon the sea"]{.class-2}[ (Gen. 22:17, 32:12). This was fulfilled to some extent during the reign of Solomon (1 Kings 4:20). So now that seed is to be carried away captive into Babylon: it's the enslavement and relocation of a whole populous.]{.class-0}

"[They shall scoff at the kings"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 10). That is exactly what happened. Nebuchadnezzar invaded Judah three times under three different kings: Jehoiakim (2 Chron. 36:5--7), Jehoiachin (2 Chron. 36:9--10), and Zedekiah (2 Chron. 36:11--19). Jehoiakim was carried off in chains to Babylon and died. Jehoiachin and his whole family were carried away captive to Babylon; there he was thrown in prison and didn't get out until after Nebuchadnezzar was dead (Jer. 52:31). Zedekiah saw his sons murdered and then had his eyes put out before he was hauled off to Babylon (2 Kings 25:6--7).]{.class-0}

"[The princes shall be a scorn unto them."]{.class-2}[ They were. The princes under Jehoiakim and Jehoiachin became ]{.class-0}[slaves]{.class-1}[ in Babylon (Dan. 1:1--4; 2 Kings 24:14--16). When Nebuchadnezzar destroyed Jerusalem under Zedekiah, the princes had their houses burnt to the ground (2 Kings 25:9), and then they were slain (Jer. 52:10) and strung up by their hands (Lam. 5:12).]{.class-0}

"[They shall deride every strong hold; for they shall heap dust, and take it."]{.class-2}[ Deuteronomy 28:52 says, ]{.class-0}["And he shall besiege thee in all thy gates, until thy high and fenced walls come down, wherein thou trustedst, throughout all thy land."]{.class-2}[ Ezekiel said Nebuchadnezzar ]{.class-0}["cast a mount against"]{.class-2}[ Jerusalem (Ezek. 4:2, 21:22, 26:8); i.e., he made an earthen ramp to scale the wall.]{.class-0}

[Verse 11:]{.class-0}[ "Then shall his mind change, and he shall pass over, and offend, imputing this his power unto his god."]{.class-2}[ Notice the sudden change from the plural in verses 6--10 to a singular here. "[And they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast, saying, Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?"]{.class-2}

[Daniel 11:36 says he ]{.class-0}["shall prosper till the indignation be accomplished: for that that is determined shall be done."]{.class-2}

[Revelation 13:8 says, ]{.class-0}["And all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him, whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world."]{.class-2}[ Second Thessalonians 2:10 says the people who follow him are ]{.class-0}["them that perish; because they received not the love of the truth, that they might be saved."]{.class-2}

[Back to the passage in Habakkuk 1. There is another reason for the peculiar change from plural to singular. ]{.class-0}[Note in Revelation 13:2 that the Antichrist is likened to three animals in one.]{.class-1}

[Frequently in the Bible you'll find men likened to animals, so many times that you can hardly blame Darwin for getting them confused (see the comments on Eccl. 3:19 in that Commentary). Christ is likened to three animals: a ]{.class-0}[lamb]{.class-1}[ (John 1:29), a ]{.class-0}[lion]{.class-1}[ (Rev. 5:5), and a ]{.class-0}[serpent]{.class-1}[ (John 3:14). He is a ]{.class-0}[multiple animal.]{.class-1}[ Do you know what the Hebrew word for a multiple beast is? Turn over to Job 40 and 41.]{.class-0}

[Now this is where Henry Morris, Kent Hovind, Ken Ham, and the Institute for Creation Research blew it. Don't misunderstand me, they all have some good stuff on creation versus evolution, especially Henry Morris. Morris and the other creationist boys took the two creatures in Job 40 and 41 and turned them into ]{.class-0}[dinosaurs.]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}[There isn't a dinosaur anywhere in the lot.]{.class-1}

[You take that ]{.class-0}["leviathan"]{.class-2}[ in Job 41. If that's a dinosaur, then it has to have ]{.class-0}[more than one head]{.class-1}[ because in Psalm 74:1-4 he has ]{.class-0}["heads,"]{.class-2}[ ]{.class-0}[plural.]{.class-1}[ I don't recall any two-headed dinosaurs, do you? The ]{.class-0}["leviathan"]{.class-2}[ of Job 41 is a fire-breather, so the creationists have come up with a theory for some land dinosaurs breathing fire like a bombardier beetle. But "leviathan" is a ]{.class-0}[sea creature,]{.class-1}[ not a land animal. Leviathan is a seagoing, fire-breathing ]{.class-0}[dragon]{.class-1}[ (see Isa. 27:1). Why, you're in Revelation 12:3, 9;]{.class-0}[ it's the Devil.]{.class-1}

[The only fellow I ever read who got that thing right was Martin Luther, and how he got it right back in 1520 when none of the modern expositors could find it, I don't know; they must have ]{.class-0}[degenerated]{.class-1}[ or something. In his hymn "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God," Luther wrote: "For still our ancient foe Doth seek to work us woe---His craft and pow'r are great, And armed with cruel hate, ]{.class-0}[On earth is not his equal."]{.class-1}[ That last part is Job 41:33---]{.class-0}["Upon earth there is not his like."]{.class-2}[ Old Martin had Leviathan pegged as Satan nearly 500 years ago.]{.class-0}

[Now what we want is in Job 40. There in verse 15 you have a creature called ]{.class-0}["behemoth."]{.class-2}[ The creationists make it a brontosaurus or an "apatosaurus" (or whatever). But that word in Hebrew means ]{.class-0}["animals,"]{.class-1}[ plural. You see that "oth" at the end of the word? That's a ]{.class-0}[plural]{.class-1}[ ending. Look at Job 41:15--19.]{.class-0}

"[Behold now behemoth]{.class-2}[ \[the word means "animals"\]]{.class-0}[, which I made with thee; he eateth grass as an ox. Lo now, his strength is in his loins, and his force is in the navel of his belly. He moveth his tail like a cedar: the sinews of his stones are wrapped together. His bones are as strong pieces of brass; his bones are like bars of iron. He is the chief of the ways of God\...."]{.class-2}

[Now up to that point you might have had some argument for this creature being an ordinary animal, but that ]{.class-0}["chief of the ways of God"]{.class-2}[ sets it beyond any hippopotamus, crocodile, or dinosaur. It is like Leviathan being ]{.class-0}["king over all the children of pride"]{.class-2}[ (Job 41:34): it is a spiritual position over ]{.class-0}["principalities\...powers\...the rulers of the darkness of this world\...spiritual wickedness in high places"]{.class-2}[ (Eph. 6:12). It is a creature second only to the Trinity in power and authority, who ends up being judged as a ]{.class-0}[man]{.class-1}[ in Hell (Isa. 14:12--16; Ezek. 28:1--19).]{.class-0}

"[\...he that made him can make his sword to approach unto him."]{.class-2}[ So it is ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ a dinosaur of any kind. If we are to believe the creationists, the dinosaurs were brought into the post-diluvian world aboard the ark like all the other animals on earth today. Any that did not die in "ice ages" or by disease were eventually killed off by ]{.class-0}[men,]{.class-1}[ not God. The creature here can only be slain by ]{.class-0}["his sword"]{.class-2}[; i.e., the sword of Him ]{.class-0}["that made him."]{.class-2}[ That's ]{.class-0}["the sword"]{.class-2}[ (see Eph. 6:17) that ]{.class-0}["is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword, piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow]{.class-2}[ \[see Job 40:18 for the ]{.class-0}["marrow"]{.class-2}[\]]{.class-0}[, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart"]{.class-2}[ (Heb. 4:12)---it's ]{.class-0}["the word of God."]{.class-2}[ That is exactly what happens in Revelation 19:11--21 (which see).]{.class-0}

[So in Habakkuk 1:6--11, what you have doctrinally is a ]{.class-0}[singular King]{.class-1}[ made up of ]{.class-0}[Kings]{.class-1}[ (Dan. 7:17---the fourth beast of Dan. 7:7 is a combination of the first three beasts in Dan. 7:4--6; cf. Rev. 13:2). He is connected with Babylon (Chaldea---see Zec. 5:11 and the comments on that verse), and his bride is called ]{.class-0}["MYSTERY BABYLON THE GREAT, THE MOTHER OF HARLOTS AND ABOMINATIONS OF THE EARTH"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 17:3--8).]{.class-0}

12 Art thou not from everlasting, O LORD my God, mine Holy One? we shall not die. O LORD, thou hast ordained them for judgment; and, O mighty God, thou hast established them for correction.

13 Thou art of purer eyes than to behold evil, and canst not look on iniquity: wherefore lookest thou upon them that deal treacherously, and holdest thy tongue when the wicked devoureth the man that is more righteous than he?

14 And makest men as the fishes of the sea, as the creeping things, that have no ruler over them?

15 They take up all of them with the angle, they catch them in their net, and gather them in their drag: therefore they rejoice and are glad.

16 Therefore they sacrifice unto their net, and burn incense unto their drag; because by them their portion is fat, and their meat plenteous.

17 Shall they therefore empty their net, and not spare continually to slay the nations?

"[Art thou not from everlasting, O LORD my God, mine Holy One?"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 12). Now this is a Jew (Habakkuk) speaking to Jews; it is a Hebrew speaking to Hebrews. So when he says ]{.class-0}["MINE Holy One,"]{.class-2}[ he is talking about ]{.class-0}[Israel's]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}["Holy One."]{.class-2}[ The Lord is called ]{.class-0}["the Holy One of Israel"]{.class-2}[ throughout that Old Testament (2 Kings 19:22; Psa. 71:22, 78:41, 89:18; Isa. 1:4, 5:19, 24, 30:11, 12, 15, 31:1, 14:14, 20, 43:3, 14, 15, etc.). But in the following Scriptures, the ]{.class-0}["Holy One"]{.class-2}[ is identified as ]{.class-0}[Jesus Christ:]{.class-1}[ Psalm 16:10; Isaiah 10:17, 12:6, 49:7; Habakkuk 3:3; Mark 1:24; Luke 4:34; Acts 2:27, 3:14, 13:35.]{.class-0}

[Over and over again in the Old Testament, the Jewish Messiah is identified as ]{.class-0}[Jehovah God.]{.class-1}[ That is why in Micah 5:2, a prophecy about the birthplace of the Messiah, it says his ]{.class-0}["goings forth have been\...FROM EVERLASTING"]{.class-2}[---just like in Habakkuk 1:12. Jesus Christ is ]{.class-0}["the Holy One of Israel"]{.class-2}[ just as God the Father is ]{.class-0}["the Holy One of Israel"]{.class-2}[; He is ]{.class-0}["the true God"]{.class-2}[ (1 John 5:20) just as the Father is (John 17:3).]{.class-0}

[Back to verse 12: ]{.class-0}["we shall not die."]{.class-2}[ The ]{.class-0}["we"]{.class-2}[ there is the ]{.class-0}[Jewish nation.]{.class-1}[ The idea is that although the Lord's allowing Nebuchadnezzar to come in and kill them and enslave them and carry them away captive, the nation itself is not going to be ]{.class-0}[permanently]{.class-1}[ destroyed.]{.class-0}

[Nebuchadnezzar came through there and did his worst, and those Jews are ]{.class-0}[still here]{.class-1}[. Haman tried to have them all exterminated in the book of Esther, and they're ]{.class-0}[still here]{.class-1}[. Antiochus Epiphanes tried to get rid of them between the Testaments, and they're ]{.class-0}[still here]{.class-1}[. The Romans came through there and destroyed Jerusalem twice, and they're ]{.class-0}[still here]{.class-1}[. Mohammed told his followers to kill them, and they're ]{.class-0}[still here.]{.class-1}[ The Catholics put them in ghettos and then tortured and burned them in the Inquisition, and they're ]{.class-0}[still here. ]{.class-1}[England ran them out, France ran them out, Germany ran them out, Spain ran them out, and they're ]{.class-0}[still here.]{.class-1}[ Hitler had them gassed, burned, shot, etc.; and they're ]{.class-0}[still here.]{.class-1}[ The Russians (both Czarist and Communists) persecuted them, and they're ]{.class-0}[still here. ]{.class-1}[The UN has passed resolution after resolution against them, and ]{.class-0}[they're still here.]{.class-1}

[There are only two nations on this earth that have never officially persecuted that Jew: they are the United States and Holland. But even that is changing. Holland is being overrun by Moslems, and if they ever take full control of the government, you will see the persecution begin. Here in the United States, the blacks have raised Cain about them in New York, and down in Fort Lauderdale (Florida), there have been Moslems protesting in the streets with signs saying: "Send them back to the ovens." Why, thank you, Adolf Hitler! That's not Nazi Germany; that's the United States of America. Right now (2010), you have a mulatto in the White House whose father was a Moslem, whose stepfather was a Moslem, ]{.class-0}[who was raised in a Moslem school,]{.class-1}[ who told a bunch of Moslem leaders that America was no longer a Christian nation (but was the world's largest Moslem nation). He wrote in his autobiography that in a conflict between Israel and the Moslem world, he would take the Moslem position. Folks, if the Lord tarries, you are headed for some real trouble.]{.class-0}

[Now, I must admit that after studying the history of those Jews as a people and as a nation, that I can understand why some Christians (both real and professing) don't much care for them. In my office, I have the entire set of ]{.class-0}[The Jewish Encyclopedia]{.class-1}[---22 volumes with 1,800 pages apiece; they have everything about a Jew that you have ever heard or knew. Reading through that entire set and studying the material in it was a real trial to me because it just made me mad. You say, "Mad at what?" Mad at ]{.class-0}[them.]{.class-1}

[Of course, if you know me and have read what I have written about that Jew, you know I am ]{.class-0}[for Israel.]{.class-1}[ I've got a sign on my door saying: ]{.class-0}["Pray for the peace of Jerusalem."]{.class-2}

[I have a Jewish flag in my office.]{.class-1}[ In fact, if you go in my office, you would find something that you probably wouldn't see anywhere else in the United States: you would see a ]{.class-0}[German flag]{.class-1}[ and an ]{.class-0}[Israeli flag]{.class-1}[ on the same shelf. So I am NOT anti-Semitic. But boy, you talk about a bunch of unsaved, self-righteous, proud people; they'll just gall your hide.]{.class-0}

[I have read all kinds of books on the Holocaust ranging from short ones of around a hundred pages to a volume of 1,900 pages; I have read accounts ranging from fifty pages to 200 pages of some of the individual camps like Treblinka and Auschwitz. When you get through all that material, do you know what conclusion the Jews come to about ]{.class-0}[themselves]{.class-1}[? I mean, here are Jews who got through the whole ordeal and survived, and they say the whole thing occurred because ]{.class-0}[they]{.class-1}[ were bearing the sins of the world and ]{.class-0}[they]{.class-1}[ were being a light to the Gentiles to show them how to live and they made it through and survived by faith in ]{.class-0}[their own righteousness]{.class-1}[ and ]{.class-0}[their own good works.]{.class-1}[ I read that and it makes me angry. I think to myself, "Why, you dirty so-and-so; after God Almighty got you clear through that thing, you never even gave Him the credit!"]{.class-0}

[Listen, I know exactly why there's a Jew left alive in this world. It's because the Lord said, ]{.class-0}["I am the LORD, I change not; therefore ye sons of Jacob are not consumed"]{.class-2}[ (Mal. 3:6).]{.class-0}

[The ]{.class-0}[reason]{.class-1}[ those Jews are still here is because God ]{.class-0}[promised them]{.class-1}[ something, and if He doesn't do it, He would be lying, and He's not going to lie (Num. 23:19). He told them they would be here when He comes to set up His kingdom (see Zech. 14), and that He would be their King, and His kingdom would be a ]{.class-0}[Jewish]{.class-1}[ kingdom with a ]{.class-0}[Judean Jew]{.class-1}[ running it (Luke 1:32--33). He ]{.class-0}[said]{.class-1}[ that, He ]{.class-0}[meant]{.class-1}[ it, and ]{.class-0}[He's going to do it;]{.class-1}[ but those Jews' righteousness and brains ain't got nothing to do with it.]{.class-0}

[Back to verse 12: ]{.class-0}["We shall not die."]{.class-2}[ Well, a lot of them did, and a lot of them will, but the nation as a whole will live. In Jeremiah 31:35--36, the Lord said if the ordinances of the sun and the moon, and day and night, could be done away with, ]{.class-0}["then the seed of Israel also shall cease from being a nation before me,"]{.class-2}[ and the same thing is repeated in Jeremiah 33:24--26. The idea is that it's not going to happen. The Lord may let that nation get down to a population of less than 200,000 people, but He will save a remnant and restore them as a nation.]{.class-0}

"[O LORD, thou hast ordained them\...."]{.class-2}[ And that's a reference to the bunch in verses 6--10. Today, ]{.class-0}[they're all Moslems]{.class-1}[---every nation in the Middle East except Israel. That's Iran, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, etc.: every one of them is 100% Moslem.]{.class-0}

"[Thou hast ordained them for judgment; and, O mighty God, thou hast established them for correction."]{.class-2}[ Bob Jones Sr. used to say: "War is God's judgment on sin here; Hell is God's judgment on sin hereafter." Israel sinned, so God sent in Assyria to wipe them out (Isa. 20:5--6). Judah messed up, so God sent in Nebuchadnezzar to wipe them out (Jer. 25:9). Throughout the Old Testament, the Lord keeps those Gentile nations around to judge and correct His people, and when He's finished with them, He dumps them and gets someone else: you see that over and over again in the book of Judges (see Judg. 2:20--23).]{.class-0}

[Verse 13:]{.class-0}[ "Thou art of purer eyes than to behold evil, and canst not look on iniquity."]{.class-2}[ Well, technically, He can't behold it with ]{.class-0}[favor.]{.class-1}[ He certainly sees enough of it. Proverbs 15:3 says,]{.class-0}[ "The eyes of the LORD are in every place, beholding the evil and the good."]{.class-2}[ You see, you've got to watch it as you go through that Book.]{.class-0}

[Do you remember over there in Matthew 7:23 where Christ says ]{.class-0}["And then will I profess unto them, I never knew you: depart from me, ye that work iniquity"]{.class-2}[? Well, of course He knew them: He's omniscient, isn't He? He knew where every hair on their head was (Matt. 10:3). Sure He knew them. The obvious meaning is that He never knew them as loyal friends or sons or followers or believers.]{.class-0}

[Over in Hosea 8:4, the Lord says: ]{.class-0}["They have set up kings, but not by me: they have made princes, and I knew it not." ]{.class-2}[Well of course He knew it, but the idea is that they did it without His permission and never consulted Him when they did it.]{.class-0}

You say, "How do you get those interpretations, Ruckman?" Simple rule: if your interpretation makes any verse contradict another, you've got the wrong interpretation. You take the interpretation that doesn't make a liar out of God; you make the "liar" out of yourself (see Rom. 3:4).

When I come across two verses that don't seem to make any sense, do you know what I do? I admit I lack the intelligence to place them correctly. Do you know what my "buddies" in the ministry do? They change the words in one or both of those verses so they'll match. If people think I'm crazy for adjusting my theology to the Book, that's okay with me; I'm never going to adjust the Book to my theology.

[Back to verse 13: ]{.class-0}["Wherefore lookest thou upon them that deal treacherously."]{.class-2}[ Now look at that: ]{.class-0}["Thou\...canst not look on iniquity: wherefore lookest thou upon them that deal treacherously."]{.class-2}[ In other words, if you can't look at a bunch of sinners, then why do you do it? Habakkuk is asking God, "If your eyes are so pure that you can't look favorably on those who do evil, then why are you looking on at this treacherous crowd and allowing them to get away with it?"]{.class-0}

"[And holdest thy tongue when the wicked devoureth the man that is more righteous than he?"]{.class-2}[ The saying is: "Silence is consent." Here are a bunch of folks doing wrong and taking advantage of another bunch of folks doing right, and the Lord doesn't say anything about it or do anything about it. Habakkuk is putting the Lord "on the spot." He asked the Lord, "Why do you do that and let that happen?"]{.class-0}

[Now that's the great problem: ]{.class-0}["Why do the righteous suffer?" ]{.class-1}[Does God know about it? Yes. Does He see it? Yes. Then ]{.class-0}[why does He let it happen? ]{.class-1}[That's the theme of the oldest book in existence: the book of Job. Job does like Habakkuk and asks the Lord why He let those kinds of things happen. He gets real bitter about that thing and says one time: ]{.class-0}["The tabernacles of robbers prosper, and they that provoke God are secure; into whose hand God bringeth abundantly"]{.class-2}[ (Job 12:6). Well, not in the long run, no; but it sure seems that way sometimes, doesn't it?]{.class-0}

[Now those are the kinds of things that you just can't figure out ]{.class-0}[down here. ]{.class-1}[I knew a Christian daddy one time who had a saved boy who got in with the wrong crowd. That kid got on drugs and missed a payment or something, so he was beaten to death, put in a car, and the car was set on fire so it would look like he was in an accident. The police caught the guys who did that, and they turned out to be the kid's own buddies. That daddy was very bitter at God about that thing, and he said to me, "I want to know something: when those fellows did all that to my boy and treated my boy like that, where was God?"]{.class-0}

[That's what people want to know: when something awful and terrible and horrific happens, where is God and why doesn't He do anything to ]{.class-0}[stop]{.class-1}[ it? I know that I want to know that. I wonder about those things just like anybody else does, and probably a great deal more because I've got access to more information.]{.class-0}

[As I read about that kind of stuff, I realize that God saw everything about which I'm reading. It really ]{.class-0}[happened]{.class-1}[, and He ]{.class-0}[saw]{.class-1}[ it when it happened, and He let it go. He ]{.class-0}[saw]{.class-1}[ some Jap back in World War II take one of our boys and pin him down on the ground and take a knife and fillet him like a deer and cut off the upper skin and pull it over his head just like a sweater with the poor kid screaming the whole time. ]{.class-0}[The Lord saw that and He didn't stop it.]{.class-1}

[Now what are you going to do about something like that? Well, if you're educated, you become an ]{.class-0}[atheist]{.class-1}[. A fellow reasons through that thing, and he says to himself: "Well, if God couldn't stop it, then he's ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ all-powerful, so He's ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ God. And if He didn't stop it, then He's ]{.class-0}[not good,]{.class-1}[ so He's not God. Therefore God doesn't exist." That's what makes atheists.]{.class-0}

Habakkuk is asking: "Why do you do this, Lord? I thought you couldn't even stand to look on evil and iniquity. Well, if that's the case, then why do you let it go on? Why do you hold your tongue and not say anything about it? When a wicked fellow devours a man more righteous than he is, why don't you stand up for the good guy?"

[But the fact remains: the Lord doesn't always do it. ]{.class-0}[Did He stop His own Son from getting crucified? ]{.class-1}[Did He stop Stephen from being stoned? Did He stop James from having his head cut off? Did He stop 20,000 martyrs in the Dark Ages from being buried alive, burned at the stake, or tortured to death? No. Well, He could have, couldn't He?]{.class-0}

[It's hard grinding that thing through, isn't it, brethren? You say, "What's the answer?" ]{.class-0}[I ain't that smart. ]{.class-1}[Of course, I can give you some reasons why the Lord does it (get our sermon on "Why Do the Righteous Suffer?") and what comes from it (get our sermon on "Not Now, But Afterwards"), but I can't analyze every single case. I don't know why God lets things like that happen. To this day I don't know why the Lord took Lester Roloff, Jack Hyles, and Jim Lince home and left us with Robert Schuller, Joel Osteen, and Oprah Winfrey.]{.class-0}

[Verse 14:]{.class-0}[ "And makest men as the fishes of the sea."]{.class-2}[ Habakkuk is still talking to God and asks: "Why do you make men like fish?" The answer is because at this very moment, every man, woman, and child of this earth ]{.class-0}[is under a body of water that would make the Atlantic, Pacific, Indian, and Arctic Oceans combined]{.class-1}[ look like a drop in a bucket (see the comments on Gen. 1:6--7 in that Commentary). So when Christ called His disciples, the first ones were ]{.class-0}[fishermen]{.class-1}[ who became ]{.class-0}["fishers of men"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 4:19).]{.class-0}

"[As the creeping things, that have no ruler over them?"]{.class-2}[ Those ]{.class-0}["creeping things"]{.class-2}[ in Proverbs 30:27 are ]{.class-0}["locusts"]{.class-2}[ which ]{.class-0}["have no king."]{.class-2}[ Men are like grasshoppers and locusts in their ]{.class-0}[size ]{.class-1}[(Num. 13:33; Isa. 40:22) and their ]{.class-0}[numbers]{.class-1}[ (Judg. 6:5, 7:12; Jer. 46:23; Nah. 3:15, 17) and their ]{.class-0}[appetite]{.class-1}[ (Judg. 6:4--5). The idea here is what is known as "the unwashed masses": hordes of people whose only purpose in life is to eat and breed and stay as comfortable as possible. If you want to see that thing, just go to Bombay or Hyderabad in India or to Shanghai in China or any big city in Asia.]{.class-0}

[Then in verse 15, Habakkuk goes back to the illustration of men as fish:]{.class-0}[ "They take up all of them with the angle\...."]{.class-2}[ That's a fishing line and fishhook, so a sports fisherman is called an "angler."]{.class-0}

"[They catch them in their net\...."]{.class-2}[ That's a cast net like you read about in Matthew 4:18.]{.class-0}

"[\...and gather them in their drag."]{.class-2}[ That's where you get the word ]{.class-0}[dragnet]{.class-1}[ in police work and detective stories. It's a net used by shrimpers and other commercial fishermen that drags along the bottom. A smaller version of it is a "seine" net (see the comments on John 21:1-7 in that Commentary): it's six to nine feet high and weighted with lead at the bottom. One fellow holds it in one place, and another fellow goes out the length of the net and walks in a big loop till he meets the first guy. Then it's dragged on shore, and whatever fish were in the "sweep" are caught in the net.]{.class-0}

[Now, both God and the Devil are ]{.class-0}[fishermen.]{.class-1}[ Ecclesiastes 9:12 says, ]{.class-0}["as the fishes that are taken in an evil net, and as the birds that are caught in the snare; so are the sons of men snared in an evil time."]{.class-2}[ Jesus likened the Kingdom of Heaven to ]{.class-0}["a net, that was cast into the sea, and gathered of every kind"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 13:47). \[See the comments on both passages in their respective Commentaries.\] They both have the right bait to catch sinners; and they can both catch them ]{.class-0}[individually]{.class-1}[ (the angle), in a ]{.class-0}[small group]{.class-1}[ (the cast net), or ]{.class-0}[en masse]{.class-1}[ (the drag net).]{.class-0}

[The illustration here, of course, is to literal fishing, but it is a picture of ]{.class-0}["the Chaldeans"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 6) and ]{.class-0}["the wicked"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 13) coming into Judah and taking the Jews like a fisherman would catch fish.]{.class-0}

[Back to verse 15:]{.class-0}[ "therefore they rejoice and are glad."]{.class-2}[ They are happy because they got a good "catch." Lamentations 2:17 says, ]{.class-0}["The LORD\...hath caused thine enemy to rejoice over thee."]{.class-2}

"[Therefore they sacrifice unto their net, and burn incense unto their drag; because by them their portion is fat, and their meat plenteous"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 16). Instead of honoring God who ]{.class-0}["caused"]{.class-2}[ them ]{.class-0}["to rejoice"]{.class-2}[ (see above), they worship their tools because they give them a good living (\$\$\$). In the New Testament, this is known as ]{.class-0}[belly worship.]{.class-1}[ Romans 16:18 says, ]{.class-0}["For they that are such serve not our Lord Jesus Christ, but their own belly."]{.class-2}[ Philippians 3:19 says, ]{.class-0}["Whose end is destruction, whose God is their belly."]{.class-2}[ This is because whatever a man earns from making a living goes into his belly (Eccl. 6:7).]{.class-0}

"[Shall they therefore empty their net, and not spare continually to slay the nations"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 17). In other words, once they've brought in their "catch," they aren't just going to "call it a night" (cf. Luke 5:5) and go home; they'll keep on killing. When Nebuchadnezzar got through with Jerusalem, he went on to Tyre (Ezek. 26:7) and Egypt (Ezek. 29:18--20).]{.class-0}

[Now, prophetically,]{.class-0}[ in regards to Israel]{.class-1}[, God is not going to let the nation's enemies ]{.class-0}["empty their net"]{.class-2}[ and take advantage of their "catch." He is going to sink their boat with all their equipment on it. The Lord told that Jew: ]{.class-0}["For I am with thee, saith the LORD, to save thee: Though I make a full end of all nations whither I have scattered thee, yet will I not make a full end of thee: but I will correct thee in measure, and will not leave thee altogether unpunished"]{.class-2}[ (Jer. 30:11).]{.class-0}

[]{#section-0_1_e7099273221ed88e_b683.xhtml}

CHAPTER TWO

2:1 I will stand upon my watch, and set me upon the tower, and will watch to see what he will say unto me, and what I shall answer when I am reproved.

2 And the LORD answered me, and said, Write the vision, and make it plain upon tables, that he may run that readeth it.

3 For the vision is yet for an appointed time, but at the end it shall speak, and not lie: though it tarry, wait for it; because it will surely come, it will not tarry.

"[I will stand upon my watch"]{.class-2}[ ]{.class-16}[(vs. 1). This is the punch line to the joke "Who's the smallest man in the Bible?" It's not "Knee-High-Miah" (Nehemiah) or Bildad the "Shoe-Height" (Shuhite); it's Habakkuk because he could ]{.class-0}["stand upon his watch."]{.class-1}[ Of course that's not the meaning at all; Habakkuk's not so small that he can stand on his wrist watch.]{.class-0}

[The ]{.class-0}["watch"]{.class-2}[ in verse one is guard duty over a city or military encampment during the night. Isaiah 21:11 says, ]{.class-0}["Watchman, what of the night?"]{.class-2}[ In the Old Testament, the night was divided into three watches of four hours each (look at Judg. 7:19 and note the phrase ]{.class-0}["the middle watch"]{.class-2}[). In the New Testament, there are four night watches of three hours each. Mark 13:35 says, ]{.class-0}["Watch ye therefore: for ye know not when the master of the house cometh, at EVEN]{.class-2}[ \[that's 6 p.m.--9 p.m.\], ]{.class-0}[or at MIDNIGHT]{.class-2}[ \[9 p.m.--12 a.m.\]]{.class-0}[, or at the COCKCROWING]{.class-2}[ \[12 a.m.--3 a.m.\]]{.class-0}[, or in the MORNING]{.class-2}[ \[3 a.m.--6 a.m.\]]{.class-0}[."]{.class-2}

[Now look at the first part of that verse:]{.class-0}[ "Watch ye therefore: for ye know not when the master of the house cometh."]{.class-2}[ That's a reference to the previous verse: ]{.class-0}["For the Son of man is as a man taking a far journey, who left his house, and gave authority to his servants, and to every man his work, and commanded the porter to watch"]{.class-2}[ (Mark 13:34).]{.class-0}

[So ]{.class-0}["the night"]{.class-2}[ (Isa. 21:11) ]{.class-0}[is the time between Christ's ascension and the Second Coming]{.class-1}[. Using the sevens system laid out by the Holy Spirit in Leviticus 23, those "watches" work out roughly to be A.D. 33--500, A.D. 500--1000, A.D. 1000--1500, and A.D. 1500--2000 (for a complete study, get our booklet on the ]{.class-0}[Seven Sevens]{.class-1}[ and see the comments on 1 Cor. 15:51 and Song of Sol. 2:13 in those respective Commentaries).]{.class-0}

[Christ told His disciples: ]{.class-0}["Watch therefore: for ye know not what hour your Lord doth come. But know this, that if the goodman of the house had known in what watch the thief would come ]{.class-2}[\[and ]{.class-0}["the thief"]{.class-2}[ there is a reference to Christ at His return---see 1 Thess. 5:2; 2 Pet. 3:10; Rev. 16:15\]]{.class-0}[, he would have watched, and would not have suffered his house to be broken up. Therefore be ye also ready: for in such an hour as ye think not the Son of man cometh"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 24:42--44).]{.class-0}

[Now we don't have to worry about that anymore. Unlike the disciples before the Crucifixion, we have the Holy Spirit living in us, and Paul says: ]{.class-0}["But ye, brethren, are not in darkness, that that day should overtake you as a thief. Ye are all the children of light, and the children of the day: we are not of the night, nor of darkness. Therefore let us not sleep, as do others; but let us watch and be sober"]{.class-2}[ (1 Thess. 5:4--6).]{.class-0}

[Not only that, but we have a completed Bible that they didn't have. In Matthew 14:25, you have a picture of Christ's return: ]{.class-0}["And in the FOURTH WATCH of the night Jesus went unto them, walking on the sea." "The fourth watch"]{.class-2}[ is the ]{.class-0}[morning]{.class-1}[ watch (cf. Mal. 4:2). We are in the ]{.class-0}[morning watch]{.class-1}[---the darkest part of the night---right before the sun comes up (2 Pet. 1:19). That ]{.class-0}["day star"]{.class-2}[ in 2 Peter 1:19 is the ]{.class-0}["morning star"]{.class-2}[ of Revelation 22:16. It's not ]{.class-0}[Satan]{.class-1}[ as you will find it in an ]{.class-0}[NIV]{.class-1}[ (see its translation of Isa. 14:12); it's the ]{.class-0}[Lord Jesus Christ]{.class-1}[ (see Rev. 22:16 in a ]{.class-0}[King James Authorized Version]{.class-1}[).]{.class-0}

[Isaiah 21:11 says,]{.class-0}[ "Watchman, what of the night? Watchman, what of the night?"]{.class-2}[ Look at the context of that in verse 9 of the same chapter.]{.class-0}

"[Babylon is fallen, is fallen; and all the graven images of her gods he hath broken to the ground."]{.class-2}

[Historically, that's a reference to the conquest of Babylon by the Medo-Persians (Isa. 21:2 cf. Dan. 5:28--31), but prophetically, that's the fall of Babylon in Revelation 18:2 right before the Second Advent. So that ]{.class-0}["watchman"]{.class-2}[ is looking for the return of Christ exactly as you are told to do in Mark 13:35.]{.class-0}

[Now watch the wording carefully in Isaiah 21:12. ]{.class-0}["The watchman said, the morning cometh, and also the night: if ye will inquire, inquire ye: return, come."]{.class-2}[ So in Isaiah 21:11--12, you have the night of the Church Age, followed by ]{.class-0}["the day star"]{.class-2}[ arising in ]{.class-0}["the morning"]{.class-2}[ which would be the Rapture of the Church. But not only does ]{.class-0}["the morning"]{.class-2}[ come, but ]{.class-0}["also the night."]{.class-2}[ So ]{.class-0}["the morning"]{.class-2}[ of the Rapture is followed by ]{.class-0}["the night"]{.class-2}[ of the Tribulation.]{.class-0}

"[\...if ye will inquire]{.class-2}[ \[I'm inquiring\]]{.class-0}[, inquire ye ]{.class-2}[\[]{.class-0}[Okay, I'm inquiring]{.class-1}[\]]{.class-0}[: return, come."]{.class-2}[ That last part is a prayer for the Second Advent just like you'll find in Revelation 22:20. The last prayer in the Bible is: ]{.class-0}["Even so, come, Lord Jesus."]{.class-2}

[When MacArthur was forced by the Japanese to leave the Philippines, he said: "I will return." He was saying that he had to leave, but he would come back to drive out the Japanese and free the islands. He was mimicking Jesus Christ. (I mean, they all have to borrow stuff from the Bible.) Jesus said, ]{.class-0}["And if I go\...I will come again"]{.class-2}[ (John 14:3).]{.class-0}

[Now the job of a watchman carried a heavy responsibility. When I was in the Army (WWII), if a guard fell asleep on guard duty in a combat zone, he would be ]{.class-0}[killed]{.class-1}[. In the old wars and the old armies, if you slept on guard duty, you were ]{.class-0}[dead]{.class-1}[ (see Matt. 28:13--14; Acts 12:6, 18--19). The reason for that is that a lot of people's lives depended on that guard staying awake and sounding the alarm in case of an attack.]{.class-0}

[The Lord told Ezekiel: ]{.class-0}["When I bring the sword upon a land, if the people of the land take a man of their coasts, and set him for their watchman: If when he seeth the sword come upon the land, he blow the trumpet, and warn the people; Then whosoever heareth the sound of the trumpet, and taketh not warning; his blood shall be upon his own head\....But if the watchman see the sword come, and blow not the trumpet, and the people be not warned; if the sword come, and take any person from among them, he is taken away in his iniquity; but his blood will I require at the watchman's hand" ]{.class-2}[(Ezek. 33:2--4, 6).]{.class-0}

[In other words, it's the watchman's responsibility to sound the alarm when the enemy comes. He's not responsible for how the people ]{.class-0}[react]{.class-1}[ to his alarm, but he is responsible for ]{.class-0}[alerting]{.class-1}[ them. If he warns the people and they don't do anything about it, then their blood won't be on his hands, but if he doesn't give the warning when he knows trouble's coming, the blood of whoever dies in the attack is required at his hands.]{.class-0}

[Now what's the application? ]{.class-0}["Son of man, I have made THEE a watchman unto the house of Israel: therefore hear the word at my mouth, and give them warning from me"]{.class-2}[ (Ezek. 3:17, 33:7). That was one of the jobs of an Old Testament prophet: he was to warn the people when God had a controversy against them.]{.class-0}

[And it's not just an Old Testament thing either. In the New Testament, Paul said, ]{.class-0}["Wherefore I take you to record this day, that I am pure from the blood of all men. For I have not shunned to declare unto you all the counsel of God"]{.class-2}[ (Acts 20:26--27).]{.class-0}

[That's the responsibility of a preacher and a pastor: he is to give his congregation everything God says---the bad as well as the good. That's the problem with "positive thinking" and "possibility thinking": it's not the ]{.class-0}[whole]{.class-1}[ "counsel of God."]{.class-2}[ If all a preacher does is give you the love of God, he's only giving you a quarter of what God said: the Gospel is 75% ]{.class-0}[negative]{.class-1}[ (see 1 Cor. 15:3--4).]{.class-0}

[If I don't want the blood of my congregation on my hands, I am to ]{.class-0}[warn them]{.class-1}[ when the warning's there. If I don't sound the alarm and something goes wrong, then I get held responsible for it. If you are reading this and you are called to preach, you need to remember that. The writer of Hebrews says, ]{.class-0}["Obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves: for they WATCH FOR YOUR SOULS"]{.class-2}[ (Heb. 13:17).]{.class-0}

[Not only is a pastor to be a ]{.class-0}[watchman]{.class-1}[ over his congregation, but each individual Christian is to be a watchman over ]{.class-0}[himself]{.class-1}[. Christ told His disciples:]{.class-0}[ "Watch and pray, that ye enter not into temptation: the spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 26:41). A Christian should watch his ]{.class-0}[Words]{.class-1}[ (see Psa. 141:3), his ]{.class-0}[Actions,]{.class-1}[ his ]{.class-0}[Thoughts,]{.class-1}[ his ]{.class-0}[Companions,]{.class-1}[ and his ]{.class-0}[Heart.]{.class-1}

[Back to Habakkuk 2:1, ]{.class-0}["I will stand upon my watch, and set me upon the tower ]{.class-2}[\[that's a watchtower---see Isa. 21:5, 8)]{.class-0}[, and will watch to see what he will say unto me]{.class-2}[ \[as Ezekiel in Ezek. 3:17, 33:7\]]{.class-0}[, and what I shall answer when I am reproved ]{.class-2}[\[the Scriptures are ]{.class-0}["profitable for\...reproof"]{.class-2}[ (2 Tim. 3:16), and the Christian needs that ]{.class-0}["reproof"]{.class-2}[ just as much as anybody else\]]{.class-0}[."]{.class-2}

[Verse 2: ]{.class-0}["And the LORD answered me, and said, Write the vision."]{.class-2}[ Now God is about to show Habakkuk something, and Habakkuk is going to write it down. ]{.class-0}["The vision"]{.class-2}[ Habakkuk records is chapter three, all nineteen verses; and as we shall see when we get there, the whole chapter is about the Second Advent.]{.class-0}

"[And make it plain upon tables."]{.class-2}[ Those aren't tables like you eat off of; they are writing tablets like those on which the Ten Commandments were written (see Exod. 31:18).]{.class-0}

"[That he may run that readeth it."]{.class-2}[ There are two possible ways this can be taken. The first is to make the writing in big letters so that anybody ]{.class-0}[running]{.class-1}[ by can read it plainly (like a billboard). That's what Billy Sunday said about his notes. Somebody asked Billy one time why his sermon notes were so big, and he replied, "So I can see them when I'm ]{.class-0}[running by the pulpit."]{.class-1}

[The other way it can be interpreted is to make the writing so plain that when a man reads it, he will want to act on it quickly or ]{.class-0}[run]{.class-1}[ to tell someone else about it.]{.class-0}

"[For the vision is yet for an appointed time, but at the end it shall speak"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 3). Now, you see that little phrase ]{.class-0}["the end"]{.class-2}[ sitting there? Do you know what's so important about that? Those two little three-letter words can clear up about ]{.class-0}[fifty verses]{.class-1}[ in the New Testament that look like they teach you can lose your salvation when you ]{.class-0}[can't.]{.class-1}

[Look at Matthew 24:13, ]{.class-0}["But he that shall endure unto the end, the same shall be saved."]{.class-2}[ Well, suppose you don't ]{.class-0}["endure unto the end"]{.class-2}[; I guess you can't be saved then, right? But that ]{.class-0}["end"]{.class-2}[ there has nothing to do with the end of ]{.class-0}[your life.]{.class-1}

[If there's any doubt about that, look at Matthew 24:14. ]{.class-0}["And this gospel of the kingdom shall be preached in all the world for a witness unto all nations; and then shall THE END come." "The gospel of the kingdom"]{.class-2}[? Why, you weren't saved by that gospel; you were saved by ]{.class-0}["the gospel of the grace of God"]{.class-2}[ (Acts 20:24). There's no mention in Matthew 24 about Christ dying for anybody's sins; it's not anywhere in the chapter. Then how could Matthew 24:13 mean the end of a Christian's life? Answer: ]{.class-0}[it couldn't.]{.class-1}

[Are you worried as a born-again Christian about the end of ]{.class-0}[your life?]{.class-1}[ Don't be. You have a promise in 1 Corinthians 1:6--8 that fixes once and for all that Matthew 24:13 has nothing whatsoever to do with that. Look at the passage.]{.class-0}

"[Even as the testimony of Christ was confirmed in you ]{.class-2}[\[Paul's talking to a saved person here\]]{.class-0}[: So that ye may come behind in no gift ]{.class-2}[\[watch it!\]]{.class-0}[; waiting for the COMING of our Lord Jesus Christ: Who shall confirm you UNTO THE END\...."]{.class-2}

[Now is that clear? You don't have to worry about "enduring to the end" because He's going to ]{.class-0}["CONFIRM you unto the end." "The end"]{.class-2}[ of what? The end of your life? ]{.class-0}[No sirree!]{.class-1}[ Look at the rest of the verse.]{.class-0}

"[\...that ye may be blameless in the DAY OF OUR LORD JESUS CHRIST." ]{.class-2}[The context isn't the end of your life; it's ]{.class-0}["the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class-2}[: it's the Second Coming. As a Christian, you don't have to ]{.class-0}[endure]{.class-1}[ till then because ]{.class-0}["God is faithful"]{.class-2}[ (1 Cor. 1:9) even when you're not.]{.class-0}

[So ]{.class-0}["the end"]{.class-2}[ is not the end of your life; it's the end of ]{.class-0}[an appointed period of time]{.class-1}[ just as you read in Habakkuk 2:3. Well, if Matthew 24:13 wasn't written to you, then to whom was it written?]{.class-0}

[That's where Christians get messed up. They don't "rightly divide" the Book, so they apply something to them ]{.class-0}[doctrinally]{.class-1}[ that wasn't aimed at them at all. There are plenty of verses in that Bible that prove ]{.class-0}[somebody]{.class-1}[ can lose his salvation---it's just ]{.class-0}[not you.]{.class-1}

[You want a good one? Here's one---Hebrews 3:6. ]{.class-0}["But Christ as a son over his own house; whose house are we IF]{.class-2}[ \[Uh-oh! ]{.class-0}[conditional]{.class-1}[ salvation\]]{.class-0}[ we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end."]{.class-2}

[Now that looks like it could be the end of life, but even if it were, it wouldn't be ]{.class-0}[you]{.class-1}[. You are not part of Christ's house because you ]{.class-0}["hold fast" ]{.class-2}[anything; you are part of Christ's house because ]{.class-0}[the Holy Spirit has put you in Christ and made you part of Christ]{.class-1}[ (Eph. 2:19--22). That isn't the case with the ones in Hebrews 3. Look at Hebrews 3:14.]{.class-0}

"[For we are made partakers of Christ, if\...."]{.class-2}[ There are no "ifs" for a Christian. You are made a partaker of Christ ]{.class-0}[when you trust Him as your Saviour]{.class-1}[ (Eph. 3:16; Col. 1:12--13; 2 Pet. 1:3--4). The Apostle Paul makes it clear that when you trust Christ, the Holy Spirit comes into you (1 Cor. 6:19) and makes you part of Christ (1 Cor. 12:13) and makes Christ part of ]{.class-0}[you]{.class-1}[ (Col. 1:27). You become bone of His bone and flesh of His flesh (Eph. 5:30). You are ]{.class-0}["joined unto the Lord"]{.class-2}[ and are ]{.class-0}["one spirit"]{.class-2}[ with Him (1 Cor. 6:17). The Holy Spirit seals you ]{.class-0}["unto the day of redemption"]{.class-2}[ (Eph. 4:30).]{.class-0}

[That's]{.class-1}[ when you become a ]{.class-0}[partaker]{.class-1}[ of Christ, but not that bunch in Hebrews 3:14. ]{.class-0}["We are made partakers of Christ, IF we hold the beginning of our confidence steadfast unto the end."]{.class-2}
