They are saying in verse 14: "We've done everything God asked us to do, and what did we get for it? ]{.class-0}[Nothing! ]{.class-1}[We've been ]{.class-0}['crying']{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}['weeping']{.class-2}[ (see 2:13), and the Lord doesn't pay attention to it and help us out."]{.class-0}

[In verse 15, God is joining those Jews and speaking sarcastically: ]{.class-0}["WE call the proud happy."]{.class-2}[ Modern preachers love that first person plural: "If WE do this and WE do that, then WE will be more like this or that." We, we, we, we, we---they're a bunch of "we" preachers. That's a duck and a dodge where you include ]{.class-0}[yourself]{.class-1}[ with those to whom you're preaching so they won't feel so ]{.class-0}[bad]{.class-1}[ about themselves. It's a ]{.class-0}[humanistic]{.class-1}[ way of preaching where the preacher gets out of calling "a spade a spade."]{.class-0}

[When Jesus preached at those Pharisees and scribes in Matthew 23, He didn't say, "Woe unto US"; He said, ]{.class-0}["Woe unto YOU."]{.class-2}[ He didn't say, "WE build the tombs of the prophets, and garnish the sepulchres of the righteous"; He said, ]{.class-0}["YE"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 23:29). He pointed His finger right in their faces and said, "YOU did it."]{.class-0}

[Unsaved people don't like that. They say, "Judge not lest ye be judged." But real preaching confronts a sinner with his sin. If you ever hear me say "we," it's because I am actually including myself with my audience. But I am not going to stand there and say, "If WE accept Christ, then WE can be saved."]{.class-0}[ I've]{.class-1}[ already accepted Him. Have YOU?]{.class-0}

[Now as I said, verse 15 is God speaking sarcastically. ]{.class-0}["We call the proud happy."]{.class-2}[ God doesn't do that; He hates them and calls them ]{.class-0}["an abomination"]{.class-2}[ (Prov. 6:16--17). But the Lord's speaking sarcastically.]{.class-0}

"[They that work wickedness are set up; yea, they that tempt God are delivered."]{.class-2}[ It's like Job saying when he was bitter at God: ]{.class-0}["The tabernacles of robbers prosper, and they that provoke God are secure; into whose hand God bringeth abundantly"]{.class-2}[ (Job 12:6). Well, that's not true (Prov. 21:7), even though it seems like it at times. That's just Job speaking sarcastically in his cynicism over what happened to him in Job 1--2.]{.class-0}

[Now do you see what you could do with the Bible if you wanted to? "Go ahead and tempt God. He won't mind; He'll take care of you." Isn't that what the verse says? ]{.class-0}["They that tempt God are even delivered"]{.class-2}[?]{.class-0}

[I can prove to you from Scripture that you ought to have sex orgies in church. Sure I can. Amos 4:4, ]{.class-0}["Come to Bethel\...."]{.class-2}[ Bethel means "the house of God." ]{.class-0}["Come to Bethel, and transgress; at Gilgal multiply transgressions."]{.class-2}[ ]{.class-0}[You better hadn't]{.class-1}[ (Amos 5:5).]{.class-0}

[There are all kinds of lies in the Bible. Sure there are: God records the lies spoken by Satan and sinners. The ]{.class-0}[record]{.class-1}[ of what happened is ]{.class-0}[true]{.class-1}[, but the ]{.class-0}[statements]{.class-1}[ themselves ]{.class-0}[aren't. ]{.class-1}[You can make the Bible mean or say anything if you mess it up enough; one of the best ways is to take a verse or phrase out of its context. When you come across something like verse 15, you have to ask yourself ]{.class-0}[who's]{.class-1}[ talking, ]{.class-0}[whom]{.class-1}[ is being addressed, and what is the ]{.class-0}[meaning from Scripture]{.class-1}[ (not ]{.class-0}["private interpretation,"]{.class-2}[ 2 Pet. 1:20) in light of the other two questions.]{.class-0}

[You take this business of tempting God, for example. James says, ]{.class-0}["God cannot be tempted with evil"]{.class-2}[ (James 1:13), so the meaning is obviously not trying to get God to sin. But when Satan tempted the Lord Jesus to jump off the ]{.class-0}["pinnacle of the temple"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 4:5--6), Jesus answered the Devil: ]{.class-0}["It is written again, Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 4:7). In that context, it means you're ]{.class-0}[challenging]{.class-1}[ God, or ]{.class-0}[daring]{.class-1}[ Him, to do something based on your own ]{.class-0}[stupidity]{.class-1}[. In the case of Christ, it was to abuse the promises of God in order to show off in front of people. That's the meaning in verse 15.]{.class-0}

[Verse 15 is God ]{.class-0}[mocking]{.class-1}[ what those Jews are saying; He's speaking ]{.class-0}[sarcastically]{.class-1}[. He does that kind of thing over and over again in the Scriptures. For example, ]{.class-0}["it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 19:24). All the scholars say that's a small gate in a city wall called "the needle's eye" that a camel had to stoop down and crawl on its knees to go through. No matter that ]{.class-0}[no]{.class-1}[ such gate has ]{.class-0}[ever]{.class-1}[ been found on ]{.class-0}[any]{.class-1}[ city wall in the Middle East.]{.class-0}

[What is that? That is ]{.class-0}[worldwide]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}[rejection of the truth]{.class-1}[ because the scholar won't allow ]{.class-0}[any sarcasm]{.class-1}[ from Jesus Christ. He wants a Christ in his image: ]{.class-0}[he]{.class-1}[ would never be sarcastic like that, so neither would Christ; ]{.class-0}[he]{.class-1}[ would never call anybody nasty names, so neither would Christ.]{.class-0}

[A perfect example of these campfire girls playing dress-up is Stewart Custer, the head of the Bible Department at Bob Jones University. In his work ]{.class-0}[The Truth About the King James Version Controversy,]{.class-1}[ he said: "There is never a particle of bitterness or unkindness in the words of the Lord Jesus Christ" (pg. 20). That's the view of a ]{.class-0}[Bible-rejecting Liberal]{.class-1}[ that's tailor made to appeal to women and little children---and a bunch of ]{.class-0}[sissified, emasculated, effeminate]{.class-1}[ men, I might add.]{.class-0}

[Your Saviour was so "unkind," "bitter," and "sarcastic" that He called the major political and religious leaders of His day: ]{.class-0}["that fox"]{.class-2}[ (Luke 13:32), ]{.class-0}["the child of hell"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 23:15), ]{.class-0}["blind guides"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 23:16) who ]{.class-0}["fall into the ditch"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 15:14), ]{.class-0}["fools and blind"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 23:19), ]{.class-0}["serpents"]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["generation of vipers"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 23:33), etc. Fundamentalists like Stewart Custer ]{.class-0}[invent]{.class-1}[ a Christ found ]{.class-0}[nowhere]{.class-1}[ in the Bible.]{.class-0}

[This peculiar tendency by the scholars to portray Christ and the apostles as smooth, slick, refined characters ]{.class-0}[like themselves]{.class-1}[ is what I call the "Pauline Obsession." I call it that because the scholars like to classify Paul ]{.class-0}[with themselves.]{.class-1}[ All of them turn Paul into ]{.class-0}[a scholar ]{.class-1}[instead of a ]{.class-0}[preacher,]{.class-1}[ and they do it because they're ]{.class-0}[scholars.]{.class-1}[ Nine out of ten of them ]{.class-0}[never led a soul to Christ]{.class-1}[ in their lives (see the remarks on William T. Brunner under Zeph. 3:9).]{.class-0}

[They try to make Paul a bookworm like themselves who would never be ]{.class-0}["rude in speech"]{.class-2}[ (see 2 Cor. 11:6 and the comments on the verse in that Commentary), so they "dress up" his language. ]{.class-0}["Offal"]{.class-1}[ instead of ]{.class-0}["dung"]{.class-2}[ in Philippians 3:8, for example (see the comments on 2:3). The ]{.class-0}[NIV]{.class-1}[ changes ]{.class-0}["the foolishness of preaching"]{.class-2}[ in 1 Corinthians 1:21 to ]{.class-0}["the foolishness of what was preached"]{.class-1}[ (i.e., the ]{.class-0}[Gospel message]{.class-1}[) so that it won't look like the ones who translated the thing are engaging in foolishness. Those are a bunch of superficial preachers changing what God said just to make themselves look good, and they do it by making Paul like themselves.]{.class-0}

16 Then they that feared the LORD spake often one to another: and the LORD hearkened, and heard it, and a book of remembrance was written before him for them that feared the LORD, and that thought upon his name.

17 And they shall be mine, saith the LORD of hosts, in that day when I make up my jewels; and I will spare them, as a man spareth his own son that serveth him.

18 Then shall ye return, and discern between the righteous and the wicked, between him that serveth God and him that serveth him not.

[Verses 16--18 are set in contrast to verses 13--15. The first group is ]{.class-0}[tempting]{.class-1}[ God and ]{.class-0}[speaking against]{.class-1}[ Him, and the second group is ]{.class-0}[fearing]{.class-1}[ God and ]{.class-0}[thinking]{.class-1}[ on His name and ]{.class-0}[talking]{.class-1}[ to each other about Him. The Bible says, ]{.class-0}["The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom"]{.class-2}[ (Prov. 9:10), and ]{.class-0}["out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 12:34). If you think about the Lord and the Bible, it's going to come out of your mouth, and if you think about other things, those will come out too.]{.class-0}

"[And a book of remembrance was written before him for them that feared the LORD, and that thought upon his name"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 16). There are all kinds of books being written in Heaven (Dan. 7:10; Rev. 20:12). The old Negro spiritual goes: "My God is writin'; my God is writin'; my God is writin' all de time." That's true. The Bible itself is obviously the Book of Heaven: ]{.class-0}["For ever, O LORD, thy word is settled in heaven"]{.class-2}[ (Psa. 119:89).]{.class-0}

[There's ]{.class-0}["the book of life,"]{.class-2}[ which is the criterium for salvation at the White Throne Judgment (Rev. 20:12). The question is, is ]{.class-0}[your name]{.class-1}[ in the Book of Life?]{.class-0}

[Philippians 4:3, ]{.class-0}["And I intreat thee also, true yokefellow, help those women which laboured with me in the gospel, with Clement also, and with other my fellowlabourers, WHOSE NAMES ARE IN THE BOOK OF LIFE."]{.class-2}

[Jesus said, ]{.class-0}["Notwithstanding in this rejoice not, that the spirits are subject unto you; but rather rejoice, BECAUSE YOUR NAMES ARE WRITTEN IN HEAVEN" ]{.class-2}[(Luke 10:20).]{.class-0}

[You can ]{.class-0}[know]{.class-1}[ your name is in the Book of Life (1 John 5:13). It is written there when you go to the Author and receive eternal life as a free gift (Rom. 6:23). "There's a new name written down in Glory, And it's MINE, O yes, it's MINE!" Well GLORY! Is YOUR name in the Book of Life?]{.class-0}

[Now there are some things said about the Book of Life that are hard to locate. For instance, Revelation 3:5 says, ]{.class-0}["He that overcometh, the same shall be clothed in white raiment; and I will not blot out his name out of the book of life."]{.class-2}

[That isn't a threat that God will blot a Christian's name out of the Book of Life; it's a statement that He ]{.class-0}[won't]{.class-1}[ if the Christian overcomes. But a Christian has ]{.class-0}[already]{.class-1}[ overcome the moment he got saved.]{.class-0}

"[For whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world: and this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith. Who is he that overcometh the world, but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God?"]{.class-2}[ (1 John 5:4--5)]{.class-0}

"[Ye are of God, little children, and have overcome them: because greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world"]{.class-2}[ (1 John 4:4).]{.class-0}

[We don't have to sing "We SHALL overcome" like the blacks did in Montgomery, Alabama back in 1963. We're not ]{.class-0}[going]{.class-1}[ to overcome; we ]{.class-0}[overcame]{.class-1}[ already. Old Michael (that was his ]{.class-0}[real]{.class-1}[ name) Luther King Jr. was a little late there.]{.class-0}

Now a Christian doesn't have to worry about having his name blotted out of the Book of Life, but somebody does. Look at Exodus 32:31--33.

"[And Moses returned unto the LORD, and said, Oh, this people have sinned a GREAT SIN]{.class-2}[ \[get that; it is ]{.class-0}[one specific sin]{.class-1}[ about which he's talking. What is it?\]]{.class-0}[, and have MADE THEM GODS OF GOLD]{.class-2}[ \[]{.class-0}[idolatry;]{.class-1}[ see the comments on Hab. 2:18--19\]]{.class-0}[. Yet now, if thou wilt forgive their sin---: and if not, blot me, I pray thee, out of thy book which thou HAST WRITTEN]{.class-2}[ \[note the past tense\]]{.class-0}[. And the LORD said unto Moses, Whosoever hath sinned against me, him will I blot out of my book."]{.class-2}

[If you take that last verse in the Pauline sense, there won't be any human beings in Heaven because ]{.class-0}["all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God"]{.class-2}[ (Rom. 3:23), so He's not talking about sin in the ]{.class-0}[New Testament]{.class-1}[ sense of the word. In the context, it's ]{.class-0}[a certain kind]{.class-1}[ of sin: idolatry. That's the sin God said He would visit on a people to ]{.class-0}["the third and fourth generation,"]{.class-2}[ and if you practice that sin you ]{.class-0}["hate" ]{.class-2}[God (Exod. 20:5).]{.class-0}

[Now what it looks like in Exodus 32:32 is that there was a Book of Life that was already written before Genesis 1:1 (cf. Rev. 13:8) with the names of all ]{.class-0}["the living"]{.class-2}[ recorded in it (Psa. 69:28). This book applies specifically to Israel under a faith-and-works setup (Dan. 12:1). There were certain sins in the Old Testament (like idolatry) that could get one's name ]{.class-0}["blotted out"]{.class-2}[ of that book and ]{.class-0}["written in the earth"]{.class-2}[ (Jer. 17:13), meaning that that person was ]{.class-0}["of the earth"]{.class-2}[ and not of Heaven (1 Cor. 15:45--49).]{.class-0}

[Dispensationally, that is ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}[you]{.class-1}[ if you are saved. Positionally, you are ]{.class-0}[in]{.class-1}[ eternal life (1 John 5:20), and ]{.class-0}[nothing]{.class-1}[ can get you out of it (Rom. 8:38--39). Spiritually, you are already in Heaven with your Saviour, waiting for your new body to join you (Eph. 2:6; 2 Cor. 5:1--4; 1 Cor. 15:50--54). Your name is added to the Book of Life when you receive Christ as your Saviour by faith and you are brought into ]{.class-0}["the commonwealth of Israel"]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["the covenants of promise"]{.class-2}[ by the Holy Spirit (Eph. 2:11--19).]{.class-0}

[The Jews in the New Testament, as ]{.class-0}[individuals]{.class-1}[, had their names ]{.class-0}["blotted out of the book of the living" ]{.class-2}[when they rejected their Messiah and traded God's righteousness in Christ for their own self-righteousness (Psa. 69:27--28 cf. Rom. 10:1--4). They can, however, get their names put back in when they too trust Jesus Christ for salvation (Rom. 11:30--32).]{.class-0}

[Although the Gentiles in the Old Testament did not have recourse to the Body of Christ like we do in the New Testament (cf. Heb. 12:23 and comments in that Commentary), God still showed mercy on them if they followed the law of God written in their hearts and consciences (Rom. 2:14--16 cf. Exod. 20:6, 33:6--7). At the White Throne Judgment, there is more going on than just the judgment of the unsaved dead (Rev. 11:18). There are Gentiles at that judgment whose names ]{.class-0}[are]{.class-1}[ in the Book of Life (Job 19:25--27); they are the Gentiles from the Old Testament on whom God showed mercy.]{.class-0}

[Now dispensationally, once the Church Age is over at the Rapture of the Church, you are back under a ]{.class-0}[faith-and-works]{.class-1}[ system (Rev. 12:17, 14:12), so it is ]{.class-0}[possible]{.class-1}[ to have your name blotted out of the Book of Life (Rev. 3:5, 22:18--19). So when you get in the Hebrew-Christian epistles (Hebrews--Jude), you keep running into passages where ]{.class-0}[someone]{.class-1}[ loses their salvation (e.g., see Heb. 6:4--6 and the comments on the passage in that Commentary). That explains certain passages in 1 John. For example, take 1 John 5:16.]{.class-0}

"[If any man see his brother sin a sin which is not unto death, he shall ask, and he will give him life for them that sin not unto death. THERE IS A SIN UNTO DEATH. I do not say that he shall pray for it."]{.class-2}

[That isn't any Catholic "mortal sin" or Charismatic "unpardonable sin." And it has nothing to do with "God's Three Deadlines" like Evangelist J. Harold Smith preached it, although sin certainly can lead to an early death (cf. Eccl. 7:17). But in the Tribulation, there is ]{.class-0}[one particular sin]{.class-1}[ that leads to eternal death, and it shows up right in the context of 1 John 5:16.]{.class-0}

"[Little children, keep yourselves from idols"]{.class-2}[ (1 John 5:21).]{.class-0}

[In the Tribulation, if a man worships the Antichrist or his image, that person's name is ]{.class-0}["not written in the book of life"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 13:8), and he is damned (Rev. 14:9--11). The ]{.class-0}["book of remembrance"]{.class-2}[ in Malachi 3:16 is a list of those who ]{.class-0}["keep the commandments of God, AND the faith of Jesus"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 14:12 cf. 12:17)---faith AND works. So unlike saved Gentiles in the Body of Christ (along with saved Jews) who are ]{.class-0}[personally]{.class-1}[ washed in the blood of Jesus Christ (Rev. 1:5), the Gentile saints in the Tribulation wash their ]{.class-0}[own robes]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}["in the blood of the Lamb"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 7:14)---works. A Tribulation saint does not automatically overcome when he comes to faith in Christ. It takes three things: 1) ]{.class-0}["the blood of the Lamb,"]{.class-2}[ 2) ]{.class-0}["the word of their testimony,"]{.class-2}[ and 3) ]{.class-0}["they loved not their lives unto the death"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 12:11). That ]{.class-0}["death"]{.class-2}[ is a particular kind of death: it is ]{.class-0}[decapitation]{.class-1}[ (Rev. 20:4).]{.class-0}

[Now before moving on in the comments to verse 17 of Malachi 3, I thought I would give you one further thing about the Book of Life. Years ago when I was first going to school at Bob Jones University, I got the wild idea that the Book of Life might actually be the ]{.class-0}[Bible itself.]{.class-1}[ After all, Jesus said, ]{.class-0}["the WORDS that I speak unto you; they are spirit, and they are LIFE"]{.class-2}[ (John 6:63); and Peter said, ]{.class-0}["Lord, to whom shall we go? thou hast the WORDS OF ETERNAL LIFE"]{.class-2}[ (John 6:69).]{.class-0}

[A man is saved by ]{.class-0}[the Bible.]{.class-1}

"[And that from a child thou hast known the HOLY SCRIPTURES, which are able to make thee WISE UNTO SALVATION"]{.class-2}[ (2 Tim. 3:15).]{.class-0}

[James wrote, ]{.class-0}["receive with meekness the engrafted WORD, which is able to SAVE YOUR SOULS"]{.class-2}[ (James 1:21).]{.class-0}

[The Bible certainly is a ]{.class-0}[living]{.class-1}[ Book (Heb. 4:12). Peter said, ]{.class-0}["Being born again, not of corruptible seed, but of incorruptible, by the WORD OF GOD, which LIVETH and abideth for ever"]{.class-2}[ (1 Pet. 1:23). Paul calls it ]{.class-0}["the WORD OF LIFE"]{.class-2}[ (Phil. 2:16).]{.class-0}

[Maybe when the Bible says ]{.class-0}["the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 20:12), those ]{.class-0}["books"]{.class-2}[ are the ]{.class-0}[66 books]{.class-1}[ that make up ]{.class-0}[the Book.]{.class-1}[ Now I don't teach that as firm doctrine, but it is certainly something to consider, isn't it?]{.class-0}

[Have you ever thought about how many ]{.class-0}[names]{.class-1}[ there are in that Book? There sure are a "mess" of them, ain't there? You know, all those big lists of names that are such a bore to read, like Genesis 5; 1 Chronicles 1--10; Ezra 2; Matthew 1; Luke 3; Romans 16; etc.? What if one of the names in those lists is ]{.class-0}[yours]{.class-1}[?]{.class-0}

[The book of Revelation talks about a ]{.class-0}["new name"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 2:17, 3:12) that no man knows (Rev. 2:17, 19:12--13). What if your ]{.class-0}["new name"]{.class-2}[ is sitting back there in the Hebrew Old Testament (see our remarks on Hebrew under Zeph. 3:9), unrevealed till you get to Heaven? You talk about a "Bible code," that would be something, wouldn't it? Wouldn't it be a dumb, stupid thing to do to erase your name out of that Book by messing with it (cf. Rev. 22:19)?]{.class-0}

[Back to Malachi 3: ]{.class-0}["And they shall be mine, saith the LORD of hosts, in that day when I make up my jewels"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 17). That's where you get the children's song "When He cometh, when He cometh to make up His jewels." The ]{.class-0}["jewels"]{.class-2}[ are ]{.class-0}[people.]{.class-1}

[Proverbs 31:10 says, ]{.class-0}["Who can find a virtuous woman? for her price is far above rubies."]{.class-2}[ Christ likened the Church to ]{.class-0}["one pearl of great price"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 13:46). Paul told the Thessalonians: ]{.class-0}["For what is our hope, or joy, or CROWN of REJOICING? Are not even YE in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming?]{.class-2}[ (1 Thess. 2:19). In the Millennium, the Jews are ]{.class-0}["a crown of glory in the hand of the LORD, and a royal diadem in the hand of thy God"]{.class-2}[ (Isa. 62:3).]{.class-0}

"[And I will spare them, as a man spareth his own son that serveth him"]{.class-2}[ (cf. Psa. 103:13). The New Testament application should be obvious. If you are saved, you are a son of God (John 1:12). As a son, you will be ]{.class-0}[chastened]{.class-1}[ (Heb. 12:5--6), but God's ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ going to take one of His children and ]{.class-0}[throw him in Hell]{.class-1}[ (Rom. 8) anymore than you, as a parent, would take your little boy and throw him in a furnace. The Lord is going to spare you from Hell because you are His son. That is because you are one with God's ]{.class-0}["only begotten Son"]{.class-2}[ (John 3:16) and are predestinated ]{.class-0}["to be conformed to the image of his Son"]{.class-2}[ (Rom. 8:29). You have salvation ]{.class-0}[right now]{.class-1}[ as a ]{.class-0}[present possession ]{.class-1}["because as he is]{.class-2}[ \[i.e., Jesus Christ\]]{.class-0}[, so are we in this world"]{.class-2}[ (1 John 4:17).]{.class-0}

[In the Old Testament and Tribulation sense, Israel is God's son as a ]{.class-0}[nation]{.class-1}[ (Exod. 4:22). At the Second Advent, Christ is going to spare the remnant of that nation, who refused to worship the Antichrist or take his mark, when He judges the nations in Matthew 25:31--46.]{.class-0}

[Verse 18: ]{.class-0}["Then shall he return ]{.class-2}[\[the context is the Second Advent\] ]{.class-0}[and discern between the righteous and the wicked, between him that serveth God and him that serveth him not."]{.class-2}[ The context is the Jews. Before Jesus Christ judges the Gentiles at the Judgment of the Nations, He judges those Jews according to their service (Matt. 25:14--30). The ones who don't serve Him as they should are called ]{.class-0}["wicked," "slothful,"]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["unprofitable,"]{.class-2}[ and are ]{.class-0}["cast\...into outer darkness"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 25:30). So in the Millennium, ]{.class-0}["many shall come from the east and west]{.class-2}[ \[Gentiles\]]{.class-0}[, and shall sit down with Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, in the kingdom of heaven]{.class-2}[ \[cf. Matt. 25:31--34\]]{.class-0}[. But the children of the kingdom ]{.class-2}[\[the Jews\]]{.class-0}[ shall be cast into outer darkness"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 8:11--12). But ]{.class-0}["they that be wise shall shine as the brightness of the firmament; and they that turn many to righteousness as the stars for ever and ever"]{.class-2}[ (Dan. 12:3).]{.class-0}

[]{#section-0_1_22445398a988daab_b69f.xhtml}

CHAPTER FOUR

4:1 For, behold, the day cometh, that shall burn as an oven; and all the proud, yea, and all that do wickedly, shall be stubble: and the day that cometh shall burn them up, saith the LORD of hosts, that it shall leave them neither root nor branch.

2 But unto you that fear my name shall the Sun of righteousness arise with healing in his wings; and ye shall go forth, and grow up as calves of the stall.

3 And ye shall tread down the wicked; for they shall be ashes under the soles of your feet in the day that I shall do this, saith the LORD of hosts.

Now at Malachi 4, the Lord takes a deep breath and says something and then doesn't open His mouth again until Luke 1 (the birth of John the Baptist) nearly 400 years later. God closes the canon around 400 B.C.

[One should not fail to note some interesting parallels between the closing of the Old Testament revelation and the publication of the ]{.class-0}[King James]{.class-1}[ Bible. The first is that after God closed the canon in 400 B.C., about two hundred years later (approximately 190--176 B.C.), some false writings called the Apocrypha start showing up out of ]{.class-0}[Alexandria, Egypt. ]{.class-1}[So over two hundred years after God's final revelation in the Church Age (the ]{.class-0}[AV]{.class-1}[ 1611), up pops a bunch of fake "Bibles" whose text comes from ]{.class-0}[Alexandria, Egypt.]{.class-1}

The next parallel is that 400 years after Malachi finishes his prophecy, you have the First Coming of Jesus Christ. If you add 400 years to 1611, that would give you 2011 for the Second Coming of Christ (the Rapture). Of course, I understand that there may be other things involved in the Lord's timetable, so I never do set a firm date like that nut out in California did (Harold Camping). But I would definitely say you can't have much longer to go.

[Of course, the Lord is going to close the Old Testament with the same theme He's had throughout the Minor Prophets. It's ]{.class-0}["the day of the LORD"]{.class-2}[---the ]{.class-0}[Second Advent of the Lord Jesus Christ.]{.class-1}

"[For, behold, the day cometh that shall burn as an oven"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 1). The Second Coming of Christ is characterized by ]{.class-0}[fire]{.class-1}[ (see the comments on Zeph. 3:8 and Hab. 3:5; see also Rev. 16:8--9; Joel 2:3, 30; 2 Thess. 1:7--8).]{.class-0}

"[And all that do wickedly, shall be stubble: and the day that cometh shall burn them up."]{.class-2}[ That's not Hell in the center of the earth (Matt. 12:40) where lost souls go upon death (Luke 16:22--23). That's a lake of fire ]{.class-0}[on the ground]{.class-1}[ in the ]{.class-0}[land of Edom]{.class-1}[ (Isa. 34). You can actually go out of Jerusalem and see it every Sabbath day in the Millennium (Isa. 66:23--24). Verse 1 is what Jesus spoke of in the Parable of the Tares and the Wheat.]{.class-0}

"[As therefore the tares are gathered and burned in the fire; so shall it be in the end of this world. The Son of man shall send forth his angels, and they shall gather out of his kingdom all things that offend, and them which do iniquity; And shall cast them into a furnace of fire: There shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 13:40--42).]{.class-0}

[The Jehovah's Witnesses and the Seventh-day Adventists both use verse 1 to teach ]{.class-0}[soul annihilation: ]{.class-1}["shall burn them up."]{.class-2}[ Even some of the "emergent," "purpose-driven" apostates have picked up the teaching. But the verse is not talking about the soul burning up in Hell. It's the body burning up at the Second Advent. Look at verse 3.]{.class-0}

"[And ye shall tread down the wicked ]{.class-2}[\[Second Advent; not Hell---see Isa. 63:1--6; Rev. 14:14--20\]]{.class-0}[; for they shall be ASHES under the SOLES OF YOUR FEET."]{.class-2}

[That's not the soul being annihilated; that's the body burning up until it's nothing but ashes. The expression when you bury a body is: "ashes to ashes; dust to dust" (cf. Gen. 3:19). The ]{.class-0}["ashes"]{.class-2}[ are at Christ's return. The Bible makes it clear that a soul burns consciously in Hell forever (Isa. 66:24; Luke 16:22--24; Rev. 14:10--11).]{.class-0}

"[That it shall leave them neither root nor branch."]{.class-2}[ That's not a reference to plant life being burnt up, although that certainly happens in the Tribulation and the Second Advent (Rev. 8:7; Joel 2:3). ]{.class-0}["Root"]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["branch,"]{.class-2}[ here, are used as they are in Isaiah 11:1---they are talking about ]{.class-0}[people.]{.class-1}[ The fathers are going to be killed right along with the children.]{.class-0}

[Psalm 21:8--10 says, ]{.class-0}["Thine hand shall find out all thine enemies: thy right hand shall find out those that hate thee ]{.class-2}[\[Second Advent---see Psa. 110)\]]{.class-0}[. Thou shalt make them as a fiery oven in the time of thine anger: the LORD shall swallow them up in his wrath, and the fire shall devour them. Their fruit shalt thou destroy from the earth, and their seed from among the children of men."]{.class-2}

[John the Baptist said, ]{.class-0}["And now also the axe is laid unto the root of trees: therefore every tree which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 3:10). About what is he talking? He's talking about his ]{.class-0}[congregation.]{.class-1}

"[I indeed baptize YOU with water unto repentance: but he that cometh after me is mightier than I\...he shall baptize YOU with the Holy Ghost ]{.class-2}[\[First Advent\]]{.class-0}[, and with FIRE]{.class-2}[ \[Second Advent\]]{.class-0}["]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 3:11).]{.class-0}

[How do you know the ]{.class-0}["fire"]{.class-2}[ is the Second Advent and not the ]{.class-0}["cloven tongues LIKE AS of fire"]{.class-2}[ at Pentecost (Acts 2:3)? Just look at the next verse: ]{.class-0}["Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and gather his wheat into the garner; but he will BURN UP the chaff with UNQUENCHABLE FIRE" ]{.class-2}[(Matt. 3:12).]{.class-0}

There's Matthew 13:40--42; Malachi 4:1; and Isaiah 66:24 right there. Acts 2 has nothing to do with the "baptism of fire." The Book has right divisions (2 Tim. 2:15), and it will never contradict if you get each passage in its right division. The only time the Bible contradicts is when you haven't got a verse or passage placed right.

"[But unto you that fear my name"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 2). The fear of the Lord shows up in every chapter in Malachi (1:6, 2:5, 3:5, 4:2). It is the reason the ]{.class-0}["book of remembrance"]{.class-2}[ was written back in 3:16. The last ]{.class-0}["gospel"]{.class-2}[ given to men before Jesus Christ shows up in judgment at the Second Advent is: ]{.class-0}["Fear God, and give glory to him"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 14:6--7).]{.class-0}

Verse 2 sets the typology for the sun as a type of Christ, which explains why the main religion among the heathen was sun worship: that was the closest thing to God they could see in the physical creation.

[No heathen in the jungles of Africa or the Amazon is an atheist; you have to be ]{.class-0}[educated]{.class-1}[ to be that stupid. The Bible says, ]{.class-0}["For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even his eternal power and Godhead; so that they are without excuse"]{.class-2}[ (Rom. 1:20).]{.class-0}

[The sun is a trinity, like the ]{.class-0}["Godhead."]{.class-2}[ It has ]{.class-0}[light]{.class-1}[ rays you can see but not feel (the Son); it has ]{.class-0}[heat]{.class-1}[ rays you can feel but not see (the Holy Spirit); and it has ]{.class-0}[actinic]{.class-1}[ rays you can neither see nor feel (the Father).]{.class-0}

[David wrote, ]{.class-0}["The heavens declare the glory of God; and the firmament sheweth his handywork]{.class-2}[ \[Psa. 8:3 calls the ]{.class-0}["heavens" "the work of thy fingers."]{.class-2}[ God wrote His words down in them (see Gen. 1) just as He did in the dirt in John 8:6--8 and with the Ten Commandments in stone in Exod. 31:18\]]{.class-0}[. Day unto day uttereth speech, and night unto night sheweth knowledge. There is no speech nor language where their voice is not heard"]{.class-2}[ (Psa. 19:1--3).]{.class-0}

[The heavens ]{.class-0}[preach;]{.class-1}[ they ]{.class-0}[teach]{.class-1}[ us something about God and ]{.class-0}["the Sun of righteousness."]{.class-2}[ What is it?]{.class-0}

"[In them hath he set a tabernacle for the sun"]{.class-2}[ (Psa. 19:4). There's the sun connected to the Feast of Tabernacles like I taught you back there under the comments on Zechariah 14:16, which see. God's a tent builder. That dusty gray tent He had Moses set up there on the backside of the desert turns out to be a ]{.class-0}[picture of the universe]{.class-1}[ (see the chart at the end of the ]{.class-0}[Bible Believer's Commentary on the Song of Solomon]{.class-1}[ and the comments on Song of Sol. 4:8).]{.class-0}

[That ]{.class-0}["tabernacle"]{.class-2}[ also turns out to be God's ]{.class-0}[clothing]{.class-1}[ (Heb. 1:10--12). It is like a seamless poncho (John 19:23) with a hole at the top (Rev. 4:1) through which the head goes (Eph. 1:20--23; Col. 1:18). Do you know why "man" wants to "conquer space"? It's because they are trying to strip God of His clothes like they did the first time (Matt. 27:35; Mark 15:24; Luke 23:34; John 19:23--24). Well, the next time, God is going to take off His "clothes" Himself, but when He does, there won't be anybody staring at Him and mocking Him then (1 Tim. 6:16). When He takes off His clothes, the heavens and earth blow up ]{.class-0}["with a great noise"]{.class-2}[ (2 Pet. 3:10): the "Big Bang" takes place at the ]{.class-0}[end, ]{.class-1}[not at the beginning.]{.class-0}

[That's the ]{.class-0}["tabernacle for the sun." "His going forth is from the end of the heaven, and his circuit unto the ends of it"]{.class-2}[ (Psa. 19:6). The sun sets every evening, and it rises every morning---a picture of the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. When the sun "dies" in the west, it's ]{.class-0}[blood red]{.class-1}[ (a picture of the blood atonement---First Advent); when it comes in the morning, it is ]{.class-0}[blood red]{.class-1}[ (like Christ's garments at the Second Advent---Isa. 63:1). In its circuit, the sun travels against the earth as Christ does.]{.class-0}

"[Which is as a bridegroom coming out of his chamber"]{.class-2}[ (Psa. 19:5)---Second Advent (Matt. 25:1--13; Rev. 19:7--9). If Christ as the Bridegroom is likened to the sun, then that would make the moon a type of His Bride, the Church, and you can get a more detailed study of that in the comments on Song of Solomon 6:10 in that Commentary. Here I just want to call to your attention that the moon follows the sun in its circuit. A Christian will follow His Lord ]{.class-0}[up]{.class-1}[ to Heaven in the Rapture (1 Thess. 4:13--18); he will follow Him ]{.class-0}[back]{.class-1}[ to earth at His return (Rev. 19:14); and he ought to follow his Saviour ]{.class-0}[against]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}["this present evil world"]{.class-2}[ (Gal. 1:3; Rom. 12:1; 1 John 2:15--17).]{.class-0}

"[With healing in his wings."]{.class-2}[ There are two things in this part of the verse that you should notice. The first is the ]{.class-0}["healing."]{.class-2}[ The Holiness and the Pentecostals teach what is known as "Whipping Post" theology. It is the teaching that there is physical healing in the blood atonement. They get that from Matthew 8:17, which is a free quote by the Holy Spirit of Isaiah 53:4. Now aside from the fact that the Charismatics are trying to wrest the signs given to Israel through Christ and His apostles (1 Cor. 1:22; Mark 16:17--19; 2 Cor. 12:12) is the fact that you don't get ]{.class-0}[all]{.class-1}[ the benefits of the atonement right ]{.class-0}[now]{.class-1}[. If you don't believe me, go stick your hand in a rattlesnake pit or go try to play with a hungry lion, leopard, bear, or wolf (Isa. 11:6--8). One of the benefits of the atonement is the ]{.class-0}[regeneration of the physical creation ]{.class-1}[(Rom. 8:18--23).]{.class-0}

[The fact is, you may get healed ]{.class-0}[temporarily]{.class-1}[ right now, but eventually, you will die, unless the Lord comes back. You won't get healed of that ]{.class-0}[last]{.class-1}[ sickness, brother, sister. ]{.class-0}[Complete]{.class-1}[ healing doesn't take place until Jesus Christ returns.]{.class-0}

[The second thing to notice is the ]{.class-0}["wings."]{.class-2}[ That's God likening Himself to a bird. The Holy Spirit came down at Christ's baptism in the form of a dove. Well, the Holy Spirit's not a literal dove anymore than Jesus Christ was a literal lamb. That was a ]{.class-0}[manifestation]{.class-1}[ (see the remarks under Zech. 3:1).]{.class-0}

One time Jimmy Swaggart was interviewing some nutty Charismatic woman who claimed to have gone to Heaven, and he asked her, "What did God look like?" She said, "He had these great wings." And Swaggart, just as cockoo as she was, replied back, "Yes, that's true; that's what the Bible says."

[It says nothing of the kind.]{.class-1}[ Speaking in ]{.class-0}[similitudes]{.class-1}[ (Hos. 12:10)---that is, pictures, types, and comparisons; NOT the actual thing---the Lord likens Himself to a ]{.class-0}[mother eagle]{.class-1}[ caring for her young (Deut. 33:11--12) and to a ]{.class-0}[mother hen]{.class-1}[ protecting her chicks (Matt. 23:37; Luke 13:34). He's ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ an eagle, He's ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ a chicken, and He's ]{.class-0}[not]{.class-1}[ female. God's ]{.class-0}["image"]{.class-2}[ ]{.class-16}[is a ]{.class-0}[man]{.class-1}[ (Gen. 1:26--27); specifically ]{.class-0}[one man]{.class-1}[---Jesus Christ (Heb. 1:3).]{.class-0}

4 Remember ye the law of Moses my servant, which I commanded unto him in Horeb for all Israel, with the statutes and judgments.

5 Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and dreadful day of the LORD:

6 And he shall turn the heart of the fathers to the children, and the heart of the children to their fathers, lest I come and smite the earth with a curse.

[We're down now to the end of Malachi, and the whole chapter is about the Second Advent. As Malachi closes his book, he tells the Jews to ]{.class-0}["remember"]{.class-2}[ some things (vs. 4) before ]{.class-0}["the LORD"]{.class-2}[ shows up (vs. 5). What are they? They are ]{.class-0}["the law of Moses\...with the statutes and judgments"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 4), NOT the Gospels and the Pauline Epistles. Once the Body of Christ is taken out of the world in the Rapture (Rev. 4:1), you are ]{.class-0}[no longer]{.class-1}[ under a New Testament situation of salvation by grace through faith. You are back under an Old Testament setup of ]{.class-0}[faith plus works]{.class-1}[ (Rev. 12:17, 14:12, 22:14). It is faith in Christ AND ]{.class-0}["the commandments of God."]{.class-2}[ That's NOT what you read in Ephesians 2:15; Colossians 2:14; Ephesians 2:8--9; Titus 3:5; Romans 4:4--5; etc., for the New Testament Christian.]{.class-0}

[Jesus said, ]{.class-0}["one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 5:18), and it's not fulfilled till He comes back. When Jesus Christ reigns from Jerusalem, Isaiah said, ]{.class-0}["out of Zion shall go forth the law, and the word of the LORD from Jerusalem"]{.class-2}[ (Isa. 2:3). Not only is the Law going out, but something else is as well: it's ]{.class-0}["the word of the LORD."]{.class-2}[ That would be the "addendum" Jesus Christ stuck on the Mosaic Law in Matthew chapters 5--7. The "Sermon on the Mount" is not a Liberal plan of salvation by works in this age; it's the ]{.class-0}[constitution of the Messianic kingdom.]{.class-1}

[When Christ comes back as ]{.class-0}["KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS" ]{.class-2}[and sits down on the throne of David, He puts out the Law of Moses ]{.class-0}[and]{.class-1}[ His own words, and His own words are the ones recorded in Matthew 5--7. That's why He said: ]{.class-0}["Whosoever therefore shall break one of these least commandments, and shall teach men so, he shall be called least in the kingdom of heaven"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 5:19). The Seventh-day Adventist will try to weasel his way in there and say, "See there, you shouldn't be worshipping on Sunday; that's the Mark of the Beast. You should be keeping the ]{.class-0}['least']{.class-2}[ of the ]{.class-0}['commandments']{.class-2}[ and observing the Sabbath." But Jesus wasn't talking about any "church"; He was talking about the Kingdom of Heaven with Him on the throne at Jerusalem as the King (Matt. 5:35). The Seventh-day Adventists created a heresy by getting the Scripture in the wrong place (see the remarks on Matt. 3:10--12 under the comments on vs. 2).]{.class-0}

[Now, three people show up in verses 4--6 in connection with the Second Advent: ]{.class-0}["Moses"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 4), ]{.class-0}["Elijah"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 5), and ]{.class-0}["the LORD"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 5). Those are the three who showed up on the Mount of Transfiguration (Matt. 17:2--3; Luke 9:29--31), and that was a picture of Christ ]{.class-0}["coming in his kingdom"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 16:28; 2 Pet. 1:16--18). Notice also that ]{.class-0}["Horeb"]{.class-2}[ also is mentioned in connection with Moses and Elijah. That's ]{.class-0}[Mount Sinai;]{.class-1}[ that's where Moses got the Ten Commandments (Exod. 31:18 cf. 2 Chron. 5:10). Moses was upon that mount for ]{.class-0}["forty days and forty nights"]{.class-2}[ without food as Christ was in His temptation (Matt. 4:1--2). That's where Elijah went after the same time period of fasting (1 Kings 19:8). Sinai is the first mountain to which Christ comes at His Second Advent (Deut. 33:2; Judg. 5:5). The "Mount of Transfiguration" was not Mount Herman in northern Palestine; it was ]{.class-0}[Mount Sinai.]{.class-1}

[One time, the mother of James and John came to Jesus and asked that her two boys could have the right and left hand positions of honor in Christ's kingdom (Matt. 20:21). Jesus told her, "That's ]{.class-0}['not mine to give, but it shall be given to them for whom it is prepared of my father']{.class-2}[ (Matt. 20:21)." Who's that? It's ]{.class-0}[Moses and Elijah.]{.class-1}[ They're the ones mentioned with ]{.class-0}["the LORD"]{.class-2}[ in Malachi 4; they're the two who appear with Christ on the Mount of Transfiguration (Matt. 17); they are the ]{.class-0}["two witnesses"]{.class-2}[ of Revelation 11 who stand before the Lord (see the comments on Zech. 4:14). For further study, see the comments on Matthew 17:3 in that Commentary.]{.class-0}

[Moses represents the Law and Elijah represents the prophets. Abraham told the rich man in Hell, ]{.class-0}["They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them"]{.class-2}[ (Luke 16:29). Paul said he believed ]{.class-0}["all things which are written in the law and the prophets"]{.class-2}[ (Acts 24:14). When Jesus was with the two disciples on the road to Emmaus after His resurrection, the Bible says, ]{.class-0}["And beginning at Moses and all the prophets, he expounded unto them in all the scriptures the things concerning himself"]{.class-2}[ (Luke 24:27). The only thing the Lord adds to those is ]{.class-0}["the psalms"]{.class-2}[ in Luke 24:44 to give you the three proper divisions of the Old Testament: the Law (the ]{.class-0}[Torah]{.class-1}[), the Prophets (the ]{.class-0}[Nabhim]{.class-1}[), and the Writings (]{.class-0}[Kethubim]{.class-1}[---"the psalms")---MINUS the Apocrypha (see the introduction remarks on the Apocrypha at the beginning of the chapter). When only the two divisions of the Law and the prophets are given, ]{.class-0}["the psalms"]{.class-2}[ are included with ]{.class-0}["the prophets"]{.class-2}[ because David was a prophet (Acts 2:29--30). Again, you are under an Old Testament setup in the Tribulation.]{.class-0}

"[Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and dreadful day of the LORD"]{.class-2}[ (vs. 5). Those Jews were told to expect Elijah to show up before Messiah came. So when John the Baptist starts preaching about the Messiah, the ]{.class-0}["priests and Levites"]{.class-2}[ ask him, ]{.class-0}["Art thou Elias?"]{.class-2}[ (John 1:21) They understood that the prophecy could refer to someone like Elisha who had ]{.class-0}["the spirit and power"]{.class-2}[ of Elijah on him (Luke 1:17).]{.class-0}

[One time Jesus asked His disciples, ]{.class-0}["Whom do men say that I the Son of man am?"]{.class-2}[ (Matt. 16:13) One of the answers was Elijah (]{.class-0}["Elias"]{.class-2}[---Matt. 16:14). The Jews had every right to expect the ]{.class-0}[second coming of Elijah]{.class-1}[, and they were ]{.class-0}[still]{.class-1}[ expecting him after the death of John the Baptist (see Matt. 27:47). In fact, to this day, the Jews set a place at their Passover seder for Elijah, and even send a child to the door to see if he is there. One day, they won't be disappointed anymore (Rev. 11).]{.class-0}

[But notice the peculiar addition of ]{.class-0}[Jeremiah]{.class-1}[ in Matthew 16:14. There is no mention of a second coming of Jeremiah anywhere in the Old Testament, although the commission given him in Jeremiah 1:5 and 25:15, 26 seems far from being fulfilled. Still, there is a strange verse in Matthew 10:23 that gives a hint as to what ]{.class-0}[may]{.class-1}[ take place in the Tribulation.]{.class-0}

"[But when they persecute you in this city, flee ye into another: for verily I say unto you, Ye shall not have gone over the cities of Israel, till the Son of man be come."]{.class-2}

[They didn't]{.class-1}[ and ]{.class-0}[He didn't, ]{.class-1}[not at the First Advent, anyway. The only possible explanation is that the verse is referring to the ]{.class-0}[Tribulation]{.class-1}[. Well, does that mean that the twelve apostles are going to be resurrected, or is the Lord addressing the 144,000 in the persons of the twelve? I'm afraid I don't have any definite answer for you, but what I can tell you for sure is this: there will be a second coming of ]{.class-0}[Moses]{.class-1}[ and ]{.class-0}[Elijah]{.class-1}[ (Mal. 4:4--5 cf. Rev. 11); there will be a second coming of ]{.class-0}[Judas Iscariot]{.class-1}[ (John 17:12 cf. 2 Thess. 2:3; Rev. 9:11); and there will be a second coming of ]{.class-0}[Jesus Christ]{.class-1}[ (John 14:3). As for the rest, ]{.class-0}["Ich weiss nicht."]{.class-1}

[Now, verse 6 is another of those landmines set by the Holy Spirit in the Scriptures to mess up anyone who won't ]{.class-0}[rightly divide]{.class-1}[ the Book (see the remarks on Matt. 3:10--12 under the comments on vs. 1). The verse is a direct reference to ]{.class-0}["Elijah"]{.class-2}[ in verse 5, but it is said to be fulfilled in John the Baptist in Luke 1:17. Of course, the quotation in Luke is not exactly like the "original" in Malachi. Luke adds: ]{.class-0}["and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just; to make ready a people prepared for the Lord."]{.class-2}

[What's that? That's the ]{.class-0}[First Advent ]{.class-1}[ministry of ]{.class-0}[John the Baptist]{.class-1}[ (Mark 1:2--3). The actual quotation from Malachi is a ]{.class-0}[Second Advent ]{.class-1}[prophecy on ]{.class-0}[Elijah]{.class-1}[. There are the two Advents right ]{.class-0}[together]{.class-1}[ just as they were in Isaiah 61:1-2; Zechariah 9:9--11; Malachi 3:1; etc. That Book is a beartrap, I kid you not.]{.class-0}

[Now what about this business of John the Baptist as Elijah? When John was asked if he was Elijah, do you know what he said? He said, ]{.class-0}["I am NOT"]{.class-2}[ (John 1:21). But Jesus Christ said ]{.class-0}[he was ]{.class-1}[(Matt. 17:12). But ]{.class-0}[he wasn't]{.class-1}[ (John 1:25); yet ]{.class-0}[he was ]{.class-1}[(Mark 9:13). So which one was he? Was he or wasn't he?]{.class-0}

[The explanation is in Matthew 11:14. ]{.class-0}["IF ]{.class-2}[\[conditional clause\]]{.class-0}[ ye will receive it, this is Elias, which was for to come."]{.class-2}

[The cold, hard fact is that in the Gospels and the first part of the book of Acts (up to chap. 7), everything was set up for ]{.class-0}[both]{.class-1}[ Advents; things were FLEXIBLE. "Elijah"]{.class-0}[ ]{.class-2}[is]{.class-0}[ right there;]{.class-1}[ the Messiah is ]{.class-0}[right there;]{.class-1}[ the Son of Perdition is ]{.class-0}[right there]{.class-1}[. Everything is right there ]{.class-0}[ready]{.class-1}[ for every prophecy on the Second Advent to be fulfilled. What happened? The Jewish leaders didn't believe the testimony of "Elijah" (Matt. 21:25), and Herod had him beheaded (Matt. 14:10) just as the Antichrist will do to the real Elijah in the Tribulation (Rev. 11:7--8 cf. 20:4)---]{.class-0}["they have done unto him whatsoever they listed"]{.class-2}[ (Mark 9:13).]{.class-0}

[Everything was conditioned on ]{.class-0}[man's free will. ]{.class-1}[IF the Jews had accepted Jesus as their Messiah, John the Baptist would have sufficed to have fulfilled the prophecy of Elijah, but they ]{.class-0}[didn't]{.class-1}[, so he ]{.class-0}[wasn't.]{.class-1}[ The kingdom got ]{.class-0}[postponed]{.class-1}[ for 2,000-plus years.]{.class-0}

[Back to Malachi 4:6---]{.class-0}["lest I come and smite the earth with a curse."]{.class-2}[ This last part of the verse ends the Old Testament and defines the character of ]{.class-0}["the law of Moses."]{.class-2}[ The Law, though it is ]{.class-0}["holy," "just,"]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["good"]{.class-2}[ in itself (Rom. 7:12), brings ]{.class-0}["a curse"]{.class-2}[ because of our sin.]{.class-0}

"[For as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse: for it is written, Cursed is every one that continueth not in all things which are written in the book of the law to do them"]{.class-2}[ (Gal. 3:10).]{.class-0}

[The Law brings ]{.class-0}["the knowledge of sin"]{.class-2}[ (Rom. 3:20), and that knowledge brings ]{.class-0}[accountability]{.class-1}[ (Rom. 4:15, 7:13).]{.class-0}

But at the same time, the Law points you to the only one who can save you from its wrath and curse because He was the only one who ever kept it perfectly.

"[Wherefore the law was our schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ, that we might be justified by faith"]{.class-2}[ (Gal. 3:24).]{.class-0}

[Jesus Christ did for us what the Law could not. Because he was ]{.class-0}["holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners"]{.class-2}[ (Heb. 7:26), He could die as the spotless ]{.class-0}["Lamb of God"]{.class-2}[ for our sins (1 Pet. 1:18--19; John 1:29). The sacrifice of Christ accomplished what no sacrifice of the Old Testament Law could: the complete ]{.class-0}[expiation]{.class-1}[ (taking away) of our sin, not just their ]{.class-0}[remission]{.class-1}[ (forgiveness) \[Heb. 9:12--15, 10:1--12\].]{.class-0}

"[Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us: for it is written, Cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree"]{.class-2}[ (Gal. 3:13).]{.class-0}

But it doesn't stop there. Keep reading in Galatians 3:14.

"[That the BLESSING of Abraham might come on the Gentiles through Jesus Christ; that we might receive the promise of the Spirit through faith."]{.class-2}

[The New Testament ends with a ]{.class-0}[blessing:]{.class-1}[ ]{.class-0}["Amen"]{.class-2}[ (Rev. 22:21). (In Shakespeare's play, Macbeth says, "I had most need of blessing, but amen stuck in my throat.") Paul said, ]{.class-0}["For all the promises of God in him ]{.class-2}[\[i.e., Jesus Christ\]]{.class-0}[ are yea, and in him Amen, unto the glory of God by us"]{.class-2}[ (2 Cor. 1:20). The Law may not be ]{.class-0}["against the promises of God,"]{.class-2}[ but it can't fulfill them either (Gal. 3:21). Only the Lord Jesus Christ can do that (Gal. 3:22).]{.class-0}

[So as we close out this second volume on the Minor Prophets, we are brought to the contrast between the curse of the Old Testament Law that will be reinstituted before the Second Advent and the blessings of God in His Son Christ Jesus this side of the Rapture of the Church. The born-again child of God is not under the wrath of the Law ]{.class-0}[now]{.class-1}[ (John 3:36), ]{.class-0}[after death]{.class-1}[ (Col. 3:6--10), or ]{.class-0}[in the future]{.class-1}[ (1 Thess. 5:9). I made the transfer from cursing to blessing (Eph. 1:3--16) on March 14, 1949, and brother, you wouldn't believe the ]{.class-0}[gap]{.class-1}[ that separated the two for me (see the Epilogue to ]{.class-0}[The Full Cup,]{.class-1}[ 1998).]{.class-0}

[If you have never passed ]{.class-0}["from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God"]{.class-2}[ through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ (Acts 26:18), you need to do it ]{.class-0}[today]{.class-1}[ before it's too late. The time is short, and you haven't got much longer before the Lord takes those of us who are saved home and leaves you to ]{.class-0}["believe a lie"]{.class-2}[ and ]{.class-0}["be damned"]{.class-2}[ (2 Thess. 2:11--12).]{.class-0}

I said YOU, not WE; there's no "we" to it (see the comments under 3:15).
